name: Computer Science & Technology
description: From punch cards to neural nets. The people and machines that built the thing you're using to read this, and the ideas that drove them.

facts.quizQuestion: Who wrote what is considered the first published algorithm intended for execution by a machine — and her notes were three times longer than the original paper she was translating?
facts.explanation: Ada Lovelace translated Luigi Menabrea's 1842 paper on Babbage's Analytical Engine and appended her own notes, which were three times longer than the original. Those notes included what is now recognised as the first published algorithm designed for machine execution.
facts.visualDescription: A Victorian-era woman in formal dress seated at a large mechanical calculating engine, writing in a leather notebook by gaslight

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Ada Lovelace — the daughter of poet Lord Byron and pioneer of computing — born?
facts.explanation: Ada Lovelace was born Augusta Ada Byron on 10 December 1815. She was the only legitimate child of the poet Lord Byron, though she never knew him; her mother insisted on mathematics education to prevent her from inheriting Byron's 'dangerous' poetic temperament.
facts.visualDescription: A formal portrait of a young Victorian woman, quill in hand, with a large mechanical gear mechanism in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who designed a general-purpose mechanical computer in the 1830s that borrowed its programming mechanism from the Jacquard loom — and never got to see it built?
facts.explanation: Charles Babbage designed the Analytical Engine starting around 1837. It incorporated an arithmetic logic unit, conditional branching, loops, and memory — all essential ideas of modern computers. It borrowed the punch-card input idea from the Jacquard loom but was never constructed in Babbage's lifetime.
facts.visualDescription: Blueprints of an enormous brass gear-and-lever computing machine spread across a Victorian engineer's drafting table

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Alan Turing publish 'On Computable Numbers', introducing the theoretical computing machine that bears his name?
facts.explanation: Alan Turing published 'On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem' in 1936, introducing the Turing machine as a mathematical model of computation. This paper is considered the theoretical foundation of all computer science.
facts.visualDescription: A stylised tape machine with binary symbols scrolling past a read-write head, set in a dimly lit 1930s mathematics library

facts.quizQuestion: Who proposed a test for machine intelligence in 1950 by asking whether a computer's conversation could be indistinguishable from a human's?
facts.explanation: Alan Turing published 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' in 1950, proposing what became known as the Turing Test. He framed it as an 'imitation game': a human judge conversing via text with a machine and a human, trying to tell which was which.
facts.visualDescription: A 1950s room with a teleprinter on a desk, a person typing to an unseen respondent behind a partition

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did John von Neumann's 'First Draft of a Report on the EDVAC' describe the stored-program architecture now used in virtually all computers?
facts.explanation: Von Neumann's 1945 report described a computer architecture where instructions and data are stored in the same memory, allowing programs to be treated and modified like data. This 'von Neumann architecture' became the basis for nearly all modern computers.
facts.visualDescription: A schematic diagram of a CPU, memory bank, and I/O channels connected by data buses, sketched in 1940s engineering style

facts.quizQuestion: Who founded information theory with a 1948 paper that introduced the 'bit' as the fundamental unit of information?
facts.explanation: Claude Shannon's 1948 paper 'A Mathematical Theory of Communication', published in the Bell System Technical Journal, laid the foundations of information theory and introduced the bit (binary digit) as the fundamental unit of information. Shannon is called the 'father of information theory'.
facts.visualDescription: A mathematician juggling binary digits, surrounded by signal-and-noise graphs and telephone relay diagrams in a mid-century Bell Labs office

facts.quizQuestion: Which US Navy Rear Admiral popularised the term 'computer bug' after her team found a literal moth trapped in a relay of the Harvard Mark II in 1947?
facts.explanation: Grace Hopper's team at Harvard found an actual moth stuck in relay 70 of the Mark II computer on 9 September 1947. The moth was taped into the log book with the note 'First actual case of bug being found.' Hopper popularised the term 'debugging', though she credited the prior use of 'bug' in engineering.
facts.visualDescription: A logbook from the 1940s with a moth taped to the page and handwritten annotation reading 'First actual case of bug being found'

facts.quizQuestion: Who developed FLOW-MATIC, the business-oriented language that directly influenced the design of COBOL?
facts.explanation: Grace Hopper devised the theory of machine-independent programming languages and used it to create FLOW-MATIC, the first English-language data processing compiler. FLOW-MATIC directly influenced the 1959 design of COBOL.
facts.visualDescription: A Navy officer in uniform writing COBOL-like English-language code on a blackboard in a 1950s computing lab

facts.quizQuestion: Who built the world's first functional program-controlled Turing-complete computer — largely in his parents' living room in Berlin — in 1941?
facts.explanation: Konrad Zuse's Z3 became operational in May 1941, making it the world's first functional program-controlled Turing-complete computer. Remarkably, Zuse built his early computers with private funding, largely in his parents' apartment in Berlin.
facts.visualDescription: A large mechanical relay-based computer filling a room in a 1940s Berlin apartment, a young engineer in a suit inspecting the wiring

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the first high-level programming language, developed by Konrad Zuse around 1945, though not published until 1972?
facts.explanation: Konrad Zuse developed Plankalkül (Plan Calculus) around 1945 as the first high-level programming language. It was designed for the Z4 computer but remained unpublished until 1972 and was not implemented until the 1970s, long after languages like FORTRAN and COBOL.
facts.visualDescription: A German-language programming manuscript from the 1940s showing mathematical notation and flow diagrams on yellowed paper

facts.quizQuestion: Who is called the 'father of the analysis of algorithms' and wrote 'The Art of Computer Programming', also creating the TeX typesetting system?
facts.explanation: Donald Knuth, professor emeritus at Stanford, won the 1974 ACM Turing Award and is known as the 'father of the analysis of algorithms'. His multi-volume 'Art of Computer Programming' covers fundamental algorithms, and he created TeX when he was dissatisfied with how his books were being typeset.
facts.visualDescription: A shelf of thick technical volumes labeled 'The Art of Computer Programming', beside a mathematical manuscript typeset with extraordinary precision

facts.quizQuestion: Who wrote the famous 1968 letter titled 'Go To Statement Considered Harmful' that helped end unstructured use of GOTO in programming?
facts.explanation: Edsger Dijkstra's 1968 letter in Communications of the ACM argued that GOTO statements made programs difficult to verify and understand, helping launch the structured programming movement. He also invented Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm in 1956 and won the 1972 ACM Turing Award.
facts.visualDescription: A 1960s computer terminal with punch-card output, a programmer scowling at spaghetti-code flow diagrams on the wall

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Edsger Dijkstra devise his famous algorithm for finding the shortest paths in a weighted graph?
facts.explanation: Dijkstra designed his shortest-path algorithm in 1956 while thinking about how to demonstrate the capabilities of the ARMAC computer. He reportedly conceived it in about 20 minutes without pencil and paper. It was published in a paper in 1959.
facts.visualDescription: A weighted graph diagram with highlighted shortest-path edges glowing in a 1950s computing laboratory setting

facts.quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'software engineering' and led the MIT team whose code prevented an abort of the Apollo 11 lunar landing?
facts.explanation: Margaret Hamilton directed the Software Engineering Division at MIT that built the Apollo Guidance Computer software. She coined 'software engineering' to give software development the same credibility as hardware engineering. Her software's priority-scheduling prevented a 1202 alarm from aborting the Apollo 11 Moon landing.
facts.visualDescription: A woman in a 1969 NASA control room standing beside a stack of printed code as tall as she is, mission control screens glowing in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the C programming language at Bell Labs, also co-creating Unix with Ken Thompson?
facts.explanation: Dennis Ritchie created C at Bell Labs between 1969 and 1973, and co-created Unix with Ken Thompson starting in 1969. C became the foundation for operating systems, embedded systems, and countless later languages. Ritchie and Thompson shared the 1983 ACM Turing Award.
facts.visualDescription: Two programmers at adjacent terminals in a 1970s Bell Labs office, one writing C code, the other debugging a Unix shell

facts.quizQuestion: Which Unix co-creator also co-designed the Go programming language at Google, announced in 2009?
facts.explanation: Ken Thompson, who co-designed Unix at Bell Labs in 1969, later co-designed the Go programming language at Google in 2007 alongside Robert Griesemer and Rob Pike. Thompson also invented the B programming language, the direct predecessor to C. He shared the 1983 Turing Award with Dennis Ritchie.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side terminals showing Unix and Go code side by side, spanning decades from 1969 to 2009

facts.quizQuestion: Who co-designed the TCP/IP protocol suite with Robert Kahn in the 1970s, earning the title of one of the 'fathers of the Internet'?
facts.explanation: Vint Cerf and Robert Kahn co-designed TCP/IP in the 1970s, creating the fundamental communication protocol of the Internet. Both received the 2004 ACM Turing Award for this work. Cerf went on to become a VP at Google, still advocating for the open Internet.
facts.visualDescription: A 1970s ARPANET node diagram showing packet routing across interconnected computers, with 'TCP/IP' handwritten in the margin

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Tim Berners-Lee propose an information management system at CERN that would become the World Wide Web?
facts.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee submitted his proposal 'Information Management: A Proposal' on 12 March 1989 while working at CERN. His supervisor famously wrote 'Vague but exciting' on the cover. The first successful HTTP communication between client and server followed in late 1990.
facts.visualDescription: A CERN office in 1989, a researcher's desk covered with network diagrams, a handwritten note reading 'Vague but exciting' on a proposal document

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented HTML, the URL, and HTTP — and in doing so created the World Wide Web while working at CERN?
facts.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web, HTML (HyperText Markup Language), the URL (Uniform Resource Locator) system, and HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) between 1989 and 1991. He later founded the W3C to develop open web standards and famously never patented his invention.
facts.visualDescription: A NeXT computer workstation with the world's first website displayed on screen: 'info.cern.ch' describing the World Wide Web project

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Linus Torvalds release the first version of the Linux kernel, famously announcing it as 'just a hobby, won't be big and professional'?
facts.explanation: Linus Torvalds created the Linux kernel in 1991 and announced it on a Usenet newsgroup with the now-legendary line: 'I'm doing a (free) operating system (just a hobby, won't be big and professional like gnu)'. Linux is now the most widely used kernel in the world, powering Android, most web servers, and all top 500 supercomputers.
facts.visualDescription: A Finnish university student at a 1991 computer terminal posting to Usenet, with a penguin figurine on the desk

facts.quizQuestion: What distributed version-control system did Linus Torvalds create in 2005 to manage Linux kernel development after disagreeing with the existing tool's licensing?
facts.explanation: Linus Torvalds created Git in 2005 after the Linux kernel project lost access to the proprietary BitKeeper version control system. Torvalds designed Git to be fast, support distributed development, and be simple in design. It is now the world's most widely used version-control system.
facts.visualDescription: A branching commit graph diagram on a whiteboard in a developer's home office, the Linux penguin pinned nearby

facts.quizQuestion: What was JavaScript called immediately before Netscape renamed it to capitalise on Java's popularity?
facts.explanation: Brendan Eich created JavaScript in 1995 at Netscape, where it was originally called LiveScript. It was renamed JavaScript for the public release to capitalise on Java's popularity at the time — despite the two languages having fundamentally different designs and being unrelated.
facts.visualDescription: A 1995 Netscape browser window with 'LiveScript' visible in a tooltip, a 'Powered by Java' badge nearby

facts.quizQuestion: What was Java originally called when James Gosling first developed it at Sun Microsystems — named after a tree outside his office window?
facts.explanation: Java was initially called 'Oak' after an oak tree outside James Gosling's office at Sun Microsystems. When they discovered 'Oak' was already trademarked, the team renamed it 'Green', then finally 'Java' (after Java coffee). The language was publicly released in 1995.
facts.visualDescription: A Sun Microsystems office window overlooking an oak tree, a developer at a workstation with early Java bytecode on screen

facts.quizQuestion: Who created C++ at Bell Labs, starting in 1979 with a project he initially called 'C with Classes'?
facts.explanation: Bjarne Stroustrup began developing C++ at Bell Labs in 1979 as 'C with Classes', adding object-oriented features to Dennis Ritchie's C language. It was first released externally as C++ in 1985. C++ became one of the most widely used languages for systems programming, game development, and performance-critical software.
facts.visualDescription: A Bell Labs whiteboard covered in class hierarchy diagrams, an 'Inheritance' arrow pointing from a C box to a C++ box

facts.quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'artificial intelligence' as co-author of the 1956 Dartmouth Workshop proposal and later created the Lisp programming language?
facts.explanation: John McCarthy co-authored the 1956 Dartmouth Conference proposal that coined the term 'artificial intelligence'. He also created the Lisp programming language at MIT in 1958, invented the concept of time-sharing, and pioneered garbage collection. He won the 1971 ACM Turing Award for contributions to AI.
facts.visualDescription: A 1956 Dartmouth College summer workshop attendees around a chalkboard, the words 'Artificial Intelligence' freshly written

facts.quizQuestion: Who called the invention of the null reference his 'billion-dollar mistake' — yet also invented Quicksort and won the 1980 ACM Turing Award?
facts.explanation: Tony Hoare invented Quicksort in 1959 and developed Hoare logic for formally verifying program correctness, winning the 1980 ACM Turing Award. He also invented the null reference and famously called it his 'billion-dollar mistake', estimating the cost of null pointer errors over subsequent decades.
facts.visualDescription: A whiteboard with QUICKSORT pseudocode above and a large red X through 'null', with a '$1,000,000,000' price tag

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented the relational model for databases in a landmark 1970 IBM paper that became the theoretical basis for SQL and all relational databases?
facts.explanation: Edgar F. Codd published 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks' in 1970 while at IBM, inventing the theoretical basis for relational databases. His work directly influenced the creation of SQL and relational systems like Oracle and IBM DB2. He won the 1981 ACM Turing Award.
facts.visualDescription: A 1970 IBM research paper with rows-and-columns table diagrams, the word 'Relational' circled in red ink

facts.quizQuestion: Who gave the 'Mother of All Demos' in 1968, publicly unveiling the computer mouse, hypertext, video conferencing, and collaborative real-time editing in a single 90-minute presentation?
facts.explanation: Douglas Engelbart's 9 December 1968 demonstration at the Fall Joint Computer Conference in San Francisco is called the 'Mother of All Demos'. In one presentation he introduced the computer mouse, hypertext links, video conferencing, windowing, and real-time collaborative editing — all entirely new concepts to the audience.
facts.visualDescription: A 1968 auditorium with a projected computer screen showing a cursor moving via a small wooden box — the first public demo of a computer mouse

facts.quizQuestion: Which Hollywood film star also co-invented frequency-hopping spread spectrum technology in 1942 — the concept that underpins modern Wi-Fi, GPS, and Bluetooth?
facts.explanation: Hedy Lamarr, a major Hollywood film star, co-invented a radio guidance system using frequency-hopping spread spectrum technology with composer George Antheil. Their 1942 patent (US 2,292,387) described the technique to prevent enemy jamming of torpedo guidance. The technology was not adopted until after the patent expired, but it forms the conceptual basis for Wi-Fi, GPS, and Bluetooth.
facts.visualDescription: A glamorous 1940s portrait split with a radio frequency diagram and patent document, bridging Hollywood and wartime engineering

facts.quizQuestion: Who developed the lambda calculus in the 1930s — the formal system for expressing computation that underpins all functional programming languages?
facts.explanation: Alonzo Church developed the lambda calculus in the 1930s as a formal system for expressing computation using function abstraction and application. He also independently proved the Entscheidungsproblem unsolvable in 1936. The Church-Turing thesis (with his doctoral student Alan Turing) formalises the intuitive notion of computability.
facts.visualDescription: A 1930s Princeton mathematics office with blackboard equations featuring the Greek letter lambda, early mathematical logic papers stacked nearby

facts.quizQuestion: Who led the IBM team that created FORTRAN in 1957 — the first widely used high-level programming language — and also invented the Backus-Naur Form grammar notation?
facts.explanation: John Backus led the IBM team that delivered FORTRAN (Formula Translation) compilers in April 1957, creating the first widely used high-level programming language. He also invented Backus-Naur Form (BNF), the standard notation for defining programming language grammars, and won the 1977 ACM Turing Award.
facts.visualDescription: A 1957 IBM punch-card deck labelled 'FORTRAN' beside a compiler manual, with mathematical equations being translated into machine instructions

facts.quizQuestion: Who became the first woman to win the ACM Turing Award in 2006, recognised for foundational contributions to optimising compilers?
facts.explanation: Frances Allen won the 2006 ACM Turing Award for foundational contributions to the theory and practice of optimising compilers, including her work on program optimisation frameworks. She worked at IBM from 1957 to 2002 and was the first woman to become an IBM Fellow.
facts.visualDescription: A woman in a 1970s IBM research lab surrounded by compiler output listings, a Turing Award trophy reflected in the window

facts.quizQuestion: Who was the first woman to earn a PhD in computer science from an American university, later formulating a key OOP principle that bears her name?
facts.explanation: Barbara Liskov earned her PhD from Stanford in 1968, becoming the first woman to receive a computer science doctorate from an American university. She later formulated the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP), a foundational principle of object-oriented design, and won the 2008 ACM Turing Award.
facts.visualDescription: A 1968 Stanford graduation ceremony, a woman in academic robes holding a computer science dissertation, surrounded by male graduates

facts.quizQuestion: Who designed the Pascal programming language, won the 1984 ACM Turing Award, and coined the sardonic law that 'software is getting slower more rapidly than hardware becomes faster'?
facts.explanation: Niklaus Wirth designed Pascal (1970), Modula-2, and Oberon, winning the 1984 ACM Turing Award for developing a sequence of innovative programming languages. He formulated Wirth's Law — a counterpoint to Moore's Law — observing that software bloat outpaces hardware improvements.
facts.visualDescription: A Swiss computer science professor at a chalkboard with 'Pascal' written on one side and 'Software bloat > Moore's Law' on the other

facts.quizQuestion: Who is nicknamed the 'Mother of the Internet' for inventing the Spanning Tree Protocol, a fundamental algorithm that prevents network loops in Ethernet bridges?
facts.explanation: Radia Perlman invented the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) while working at Digital Equipment Corporation. STP is essential to the operation of network bridges and Ethernet networks, preventing broadcast storms by creating a loop-free logical topology. Despite the nickname 'Mother of the Internet', Perlman has said the Internet is a collective achievement.
facts.visualDescription: A network diagram of Ethernet bridges with a spanning tree highlighted in green, routing data around a broken loop

facts.quizQuestion: Who co-authored the 1980 textbook 'Introduction to VLSI Systems' that revolutionised chip design education and helped a generation of engineers design integrated circuits?
facts.explanation: Lynn Conway co-authored 'Introduction to VLSI Systems' with Carver Mead in 1980, which established the Mead-Conway VLSI design rules and transformed how integrated circuits were taught and designed. Earlier, while at IBM, she had invented superscalar dynamic instruction handling — a key technology for modern out-of-order CPUs — before IBM fired her when she disclosed her gender transition.
facts.visualDescription: A 1980 engineering textbook open to VLSI chip layout diagrams, beside a modern CPU showing billions of transistors at nanometre scale

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Geoffrey Hinton — 'the Godfather of AI' — share the Nobel Prize in Physics for discoveries enabling machine learning with artificial neural networks?
facts.explanation: Geoffrey Hinton and John Hopfield shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics for foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks. Hinton, known as 'the Godfather of AI', had left Google in 2023 to speak more freely about AI risks.
facts.visualDescription: A Nobel Prize medal beside a diagram of a deep neural network, surrounded by headlines about AI breakthroughs from 2024

facts.quizQuestion: Which Stanford PhD student co-invented the PageRank algorithm that became the foundation of Google's search engine?
facts.explanation: Larry Page co-invented the PageRank algorithm with Sergey Brin while both were PhD students at Stanford. PageRank ranked web pages by the number and quality of links pointing to them. Page and Brin co-founded Google, incorporated on 4 September 1998.
facts.visualDescription: A Stanford University computer lab in 1998, a whiteboard showing a directed graph of web pages with scores, 'PageRank' written in blue marker

facts.quizQuestion: Who created Python and served as its 'benevolent dictator for life' until stepping down in 2018?
facts.explanation: Guido van Rossum created Python in the late 1980s, releasing version 0.9.0 in 1991. He was Python's 'benevolent dictator for life' (BDFL) — holding final decision authority over the language's direction — until he stepped down on 12 July 2018. Python became one of the most popular languages in the world.
facts.visualDescription: A Dutch programmer surrounded by Python documentation and a 'BDFL' crown, stepping away from a throne made of Python manuals

facts.quizQuestion: Which British engineer designed the Colossus — the world's first programmable electronic digital computer, built to break Nazi Germany's Lorenz cipher — that remained classified until the 1970s?
facts.explanation: Tommy Flowers designed the Colossus computer, which became operational in February 1944 at Bletchley Park. Built to decrypt Lorenz cipher messages, it used 1,500 vacuum tubes and was programmable via switches and plugboards. Its existence was kept classified until the 1970s, contributing to the myth that ENIAC was the first electronic computer.
facts.visualDescription: A 1944 Bletchley Park computing room filled with rows of vacuum tubes and spinning paper tape reels, the Lorenz cipher machine visible on a nearby table

facts.quizQuestion: What does ENIAC stand for?
facts.explanation: ENIAC stands for Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer. Completed in 1945 at the University of Pennsylvania, it was designed by John Mauchly and J. Presper Eckert to calculate artillery firing tables for the US Army. It contained approximately 18,000 vacuum tubes and weighed over 30 tons.
facts.visualDescription: The ENIAC computer filling an entire room at the University of Pennsylvania, engineers adjusting cables and vacuum tube panels

facts.quizQuestion: Who co-designed ENIAC with John Mauchly — a machine that weighed over 30 tons, occupied 300 square feet, and consumed 150 kilowatts of electricity?
facts.explanation: J. Presper Eckert co-designed ENIAC with John Mauchly at the University of Pennsylvania. The machine weighed more than 30 short tons, occupied 300 square feet (about a third of an average American home), and consumed 150 kW of electricity. Its first program was a feasibility study for the hydrogen bomb.
facts.visualDescription: Photograph of ENIAC's enormous wall of vacuum tubes and wiring, engineers dwarfed by the machine, punch-card trays stacked nearby

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did John Bardeen and Walter Brattain conduct the critical experiment at Bell Labs demonstrating the world's first working transistor?
facts.explanation: The critical transistor experiment was carried out on 16 December 1947 by John Bardeen and Walter Brattain, working under William Shockley's group at Bell Laboratories. Using a block of germanium, they demonstrated amplification. Bardeen, Brattain, and Shockley shared the 1956 Nobel Prize in Physics for the invention.
facts.visualDescription: A Bell Labs laboratory bench in 1947 with a small germanium block, fine gold foils, and oscilloscope showing the first amplified signal

facts.quizQuestion: What electronic component did the transistor replace, making computers smaller, faster, cooler, and far more reliable?
facts.explanation: The transistor replaced the vacuum tube (thermionic valve) as the fundamental active electronic component. Vacuum tubes were large, generated enormous heat, burned out frequently, and consumed huge amounts of power. ENIAC used approximately 18,000 vacuum tubes requiring constant maintenance.
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison: a glowing glass vacuum tube the size of a fist versus a transistor the size of a thumbnail, with 'REPLACED' in bold

facts.quizQuestion: Which computer famously and correctly predicted Eisenhower's victory in the 1952 US presidential election for CBS News — though the broadcast network initially refused to air the result, doubting the computer?
facts.explanation: UNIVAC I (Universal Automatic Computer) correctly predicted Eisenhower's landslide victory in the 1952 presidential election for CBS News. With only 5% of votes counted, it gave Eisenhower 438 electoral votes. CBS initially refused to broadcast the result, believing the computer must be wrong. UNIVAC was right.
facts.visualDescription: A 1952 CBS television studio with camera operators around a massive computer, a stunned newscaster holding a printout reading 'Eisenhower 438 electoral votes'

facts.quizQuestion: Which computer this anatomical structure stores program instructions and data in the same memory, allowing programs to modify themselves — and is used by the vast majority of modern computers?
facts.explanation: The von Neumann architecture (also called Princeton architecture) stores instructions and data in the same shared memory, with a single bus connecting the CPU to memory. This allows programs to be treated and modified like data. It contrasts with the Harvard architecture, which uses physically separate instruction and data memory.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram showing a CPU connected to a single unified memory block containing both 'PROGRAM' and 'DATA' sections, with bidirectional arrows

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Jack Kilby demonstrate the first working integrated circuit at Texas Instruments, launching the age of miniaturised electronics?
facts.explanation: Jack Kilby demonstrated the first working integrated circuit on 12 September 1958 at Texas Instruments. As a new employee without earned vacation time, he used the summer to work on miniaturisation, building a circuit that combined transistors, resistors, and a capacitor on a single piece of germanium. Kilby shared the 2000 Nobel Prize in Physics for this invention.
facts.visualDescription: A Texas Instruments laboratory in 1958, a small semiconductor chip on a glass slide under a microscope, the engineer noting results in a lab book

facts.quizQuestion: Who won the 2000 Nobel Prize in Physics for the invention of the integrated circuit — despite a simultaneous independent invention by Robert Noyce at Fairchild Semiconductor?
facts.explanation: Jack Kilby shared the 2000 Nobel Prize in Physics for the invention of the integrated circuit. Robert Noyce independently developed a silicon-based IC at Fairchild Semiconductor in 1959 (Kilby used germanium). Noyce died in 1990 and Nobel Prizes are not awarded posthumously. Noyce co-founded Intel and his silicon-based approach became the industry standard.
facts.visualDescription: Two side-by-side laboratory photos: Kilby at Texas Instruments with a germanium chip, Noyce at Fairchild with a silicon wafer, both in 1958-1959

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Gordon Moore publish the paper observing that transistor counts in integrated circuits had doubled annually — the observation later named Moore's Law?
facts.explanation: Gordon Moore published 'Cramming more components onto integrated circuits' in Electronics magazine on 19 April 1965. He observed transistor count had doubled annually and projected this would continue. He revised this in 1975 to doubling approximately every two years. Caltech professor Carver Mead named it Moore's Law.
facts.visualDescription: A 1965 Electronics magazine open to Moore's graph plotting transistor counts over time, showing an exponential curve

facts.quizQuestion: Which Intel co-founder proposed the observation that transistor counts on integrated circuits double roughly every two years?
facts.explanation: Gordon Moore co-founded Intel in 1968 with Robert Noyce, previously having co-founded Fairchild Semiconductor in 1957. His 1965 paper in Electronics magazine contained what became known as Moore's Law. He served as Intel's CEO from 1975 to 1987.
facts.visualDescription: An Intel executive at a podium beside a chart showing exponential transistor growth curves from 1965 to 2020

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Intel release the 4004, the world's first commercially available single-chip microprocessor?
facts.explanation: Intel released the 4004 on 15 November 1971, priced at US$60. The 4-bit CPU contained 2,300 transistors in a 10-micrometre process and ran at 740 kHz. It was designed by Federico Faggin, Ted Hoff, Stanley Mazor, and Masatoshi Shima, originally for Nippon Calculating Machine Corporation's Busicom calculator.
facts.visualDescription: The tiny Intel 4004 chip on a fingertip, beside a Busicom calculator from 1971 that was its original home

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the first commercially available single-chip microprocessor, released by Intel in 1971 with just 2,300 transistors — compared to billions in modern CPUs?
facts.explanation: The Intel 4004, released 15 November 1971, was the first commercially available single-chip microprocessor. It contained 2,300 transistors in a 4-bit design clocked at 740 kHz. Modern processors contain tens of billions of transistors — a factor of roughly 10 million improvement, consistent with Moore's Law.
facts.visualDescription: A magnified view of the Intel 4004 die showing its 2,300 transistors, beside a modern processor diagram with tens of billions

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did IBM introduce the first commercial hard disk drive — the IBM 350 — which stored a whopping 5 MB of data on 50 platters each 24 inches in diameter?
facts.explanation: IBM publicly announced the IBM 305 RAMAC (Random Access Method of Accounting and Control) on 14 September 1956. Its hard disk unit, the IBM 350, stored 5 megabytes on 50 platters each 24 inches in diameter. The entire unit weighed over a ton and was the size of two refrigerators. Modern drives store terabytes in a device the size of a paperback book.
facts.visualDescription: Two refrigerator-sized IBM 305 RAMAC disk units from 1956, with 50 massive 24-inch platters inside, an IBM engineer in a suit standing beside them

facts.quizQuestion: What was the diameter (in inches) of IBM's first floppy disk, invented in 1971?
facts.explanation: The first floppy disks were invented by IBM in 1971 with a diameter of 8 inches. The format evolved through the 5¼-inch disk to the 3½-inch format introduced by Sony in 1981. The 3½-inch floppy became the standard for personal computers through the 1980s and 1990s before being replaced by USB flash drives.
facts.visualDescription: Three floppy disks side by side in decreasing sizes: 8-inch, 5.25-inch, and 3.5-inch, with the year of introduction below each

facts.quizQuestion: Who was the electrical genius who designed the Apple I in 1976 — first to include video display circuitry on the board, allowing connection to a regular TV?
facts.explanation: Steve Wozniak electrically designed the Apple I in 1976. Its key innovation was including video display terminal circuitry directly on the board, allowing it to connect to a standard TV rather than requiring an expensive dedicated display. Steve Jobs handled marketing and company formation. Only about 200 units were built, originally sold for $666.66.
facts.visualDescription: A bare wooden circuit board — the Apple I — beside a 1970s television set, both connected with a cable, in a Cupertino garage

facts.quizQuestion: What 1979 spreadsheet program — widely called the first 'killer app' — ran on the Apple II and drove the computer's massive commercial success?
facts.explanation: VisiCalc (1979), developed by Dan Bricklin and Bob Frankston, was the first spreadsheet program for personal computers and ran on the Apple II. It was the first software that gave businesses a compelling reason to buy a personal computer. Often called the first 'killer app', VisiCalc transformed the Apple II from a hobbyist toy into a business tool.
facts.visualDescription: An Apple II computer in a 1979 office, its green-phosphor screen displaying a spreadsheet grid, an accountant replacing their paper ledger with the computer

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did IBM release its first Personal Computer (model 5150), establishing the open architecture that defined the PC industry for decades?
facts.explanation: IBM released its Personal Computer (model 5150) on 12 August 1981. Directed by William Lowe and Philip Don Estridge at IBM Boca Raton, it used an open architecture based on the Intel 8088 and licensed Microsoft's PC-DOS. The open architecture allowed clone manufacturers like Compaq to build compatible machines, eventually commoditising the entire PC market.
facts.visualDescription: The 1981 IBM PC 5150 on a office desk, a keyboard in front and a greenscreen monitor beside it, the iconic IBM logo on the beige case

facts.quizQuestion: Who built the first computer mouse prototype in 1964 at SRI International — a small wooden box with two wheels for tracking X and Y movement?
facts.explanation: Douglas Engelbart and engineer Bill English built the first computer mouse prototype in 1964 at SRI International. The original mouse was a small wooden shell containing two wheels for tracking X and Y movement. It was first publicly demonstrated at Engelbart's famous 1968 'Mother of All Demos'.
facts.visualDescription: The original 1964 wooden computer mouse prototype with two small metal wheels visible underneath, sitting on a drafting table beside early SRI computing equipment

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented DRAM in 1966 at IBM, solving how to store one bit of data in a single transistor-capacitor cell — making high-density RAM economically practical?
facts.explanation: Dr. Robert Dennard invented DRAM (Dynamic Random-Access Memory) in 1966 at IBM's Thomas J. Watson Research Center, filing his patent in 1967. A DRAM cell stores one bit using a single MOS transistor and a capacitor. DRAM became and remains the dominant type of RAM used as main memory in computers. Dennard also formulated Dennard scaling rules.
facts.visualDescription: A 1960s IBM research lab with circuit diagrams showing a single transistor-capacitor memory cell, the word 'DRAM' written above the schematic

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did SunDisk (later SanDisk) introduce the first commercial flash-based solid-state drive with a capacity of 20 MB?
facts.explanation: SunDisk (later renamed SanDisk) introduced the first commercial flash-based SSD in 1991 with 20 MB capacity. SSDs use non-volatile NAND flash memory with no moving parts, enabling much faster random access than HDDs. NAND flash itself was invented by Fujio Masuoka at Toshiba in 1984. SSDs became mainstream in laptops from around 2010.
facts.visualDescription: A 1991 SunDisk flash storage card beside a hard disk drive of the same era, emphasising the absence of spinning platters

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the first product NVIDIA called a 'GPU', released in 1999 — the first 3D accelerator with hardware transform and lighting?
facts.explanation: NVIDIA's GeForce 256, released 11 October 1999, was the first product NVIDIA called a GPU (Graphics Processing Unit) — a term NVIDIA coined. It was the first 3D accelerator to include a hardware transform and lighting (T&L) engine, offloading geometry calculations from the CPU. The GPU concept evolved from fixed-function to programmable shaders to general-purpose GPU computing (GPGPU).
facts.visualDescription: The NVIDIA GeForce 256 graphics card from 1999 beside a first-generation GPU diagram, with hardware T&L block highlighted

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did John Ambrose Fleming invent the thermionic diode vacuum tube — the first practical electronic valve?
facts.explanation: John Ambrose Fleming invented the thermionic diode in 1904, creating the first practical electronic valve. The triode (with a control grid) followed in 1906, invented by Lee de Forest. Vacuum tubes were the sole active electronic components in the first generation of electronic computers until being replaced by transistors from the mid-1950s.
facts.visualDescription: A glowing glass vacuum tube from the early 1900s, its filament orange-hot inside an evacuated glass envelope, mounted on a Bakelite base

facts.quizQuestion: What scaling rule — formulated in 1974 by Robert Dennard — held that as transistors shrank, power density stayed constant, enabling decades of CPU clock speed increases, until it broke down around 2006?
facts.explanation: Dennard scaling, formulated by Robert Dennard in 1974, held that as transistors get smaller, power density stays constant — allowing faster and more powerful chips without increased power consumption. This enabled the CPU clock speed race of the 1990s and early 2000s. Around 2006, Dennard scaling broke down due to leakage current and thermal limits, forcing the industry to shift from faster single cores to multi-core designs.
facts.visualDescription: A chart showing CPU clock speeds rising steeply until 2006 then flattening, while core count rises, illustrating the shift from Dennard scaling to multi-core

facts.quizQuestion: When IBM needed an operating system for its 1981 Personal Computer, which company licensed PC-DOS to IBM — making that company's fortune in the process?
facts.explanation: IBM licensed PC-DOS (MS-DOS) from Microsoft for the IBM PC in 1981. Critically, Microsoft retained the right to license MS-DOS to other manufacturers. When clone makers like Compaq built IBM-compatible PCs, they also needed MS-DOS — making Microsoft the dominant operating system supplier for the entire PC industry. This deal made Microsoft's fortune.
facts.visualDescription: A 1981 IBM PC running DOS, with a Microsoft logo visible on the software packaging stacked beside it

facts.quizQuestion: Which computer this anatomical structure uses physically separate memory for instructions and data — allowing both to be accessed simultaneously — named after the university where its earliest example was built?
facts.explanation: The Harvard architecture uses physically separate storage and signal pathways for instructions and data, allowing both to be accessed simultaneously for improved performance. It is named after the Harvard Mark I (operational 1944), which stored instructions on punched tape separate from data in electro-mechanical counters. It is used today in microcontrollers, DSPs, and embedded systems.
facts.visualDescription: An architecture diagram showing two separate memory blocks — one for 'INSTRUCTIONS' and one for 'DATA' — each connected to a CPU by independent buses

facts.quizQuestion: What type of component defines the fourth generation of computing (1971 to present), integrating an entire CPU onto a single chip for the first time?
facts.explanation: The fourth generation of computing (1971-present) is defined by the microprocessor: an entire CPU on a single integrated circuit chip. The Intel 4004 (1971) was the first commercial example. Transistor count per chip has grown from 2,300 (Intel 4004) to tens of billions in modern processors, consistent with Moore's Law.
facts.visualDescription: A timeline showing vacuum tube, transistor, integrated circuit, and microprocessor as four generations, with the Intel 4004 representing the fourth era

facts.quizQuestion: Which semiconductor company did Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore co-found in 1968 — going on to create the world's first commercial microprocessor?
facts.explanation: Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore co-founded Intel Corporation in 1968, having previously co-founded Fairchild Semiconductor in 1957. Intel went on to release the Intel 4004 in 1971, the first commercially available single-chip microprocessor. Noyce was nicknamed 'the Mayor of Silicon Valley' for his role in creating the semiconductor industry.
facts.visualDescription: The Intel corporate headquarters in Santa Clara, California, with the Intel 'dropped-e' logo, a wafer of microprocessors in the foreground

facts.quizQuestion: What type of programming language first allowed programmers to write human-readable mnemonics like MOV and ADD instead of raw binary machine code — first described in Kathleen and Andrew Booth's 1947 work?
facts.explanation: The use of symbolic language to represent machine code instructions was first found in Kathleen and Andrew Donald Booth's 1947 work 'Coding for A.R.C.' Assembly language uses mnemonics (MOV, ADD, JMP) to represent binary CPU instructions. The term 'assembler' was coined by Wilkes, Wheeler, and Gill in 1951. FORTRAN (1957) was the first widely used alternative to assembly.
facts.visualDescription: A 1950s printout of assembly code listing machine mnemonics like MOV, ADD, and JMP beside their binary equivalents

facts.quizQuestion: What does FORTRAN stand for?
facts.explanation: FORTRAN stands for Formula Translation (or FORmula TRANslation). Led by John Backus at IBM, the first compilers that produced accurate code were delivered in April 1957. FORTRAN was the first widely used high-level programming language, making scientific programming accessible without assembly. It is still used today in scientific and high-performance computing.
facts.visualDescription: A 1957 IBM punch-card deck labelled 'FORTRAN PROGRAM', with mathematical formulas being 'translated' into machine code on a diagram
distractors: Fast Operational Translator for Algebraic Notation
Formulated Notation for Technical Analysis and Research
Functional Operations Translator for Algebraic Routines

facts.quizQuestion: Who led the IBM team that created FORTRAN in 1957, producing the first widely used high-level programming language?
facts.explanation: John Backus led the IBM team that created FORTRAN, with the first compilers delivering accurate code in April 1957. Backus also invented Backus-Naur Form (BNF), the standard notation for defining programming language grammars. He won the 1977 ACM Turing Award.
facts.visualDescription: An IBM research team in 1957 around a FORTRAN compiler listing, scientific equations and punch card decks surrounding them

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented the Lisp programming language at MIT in 1958, creating the second-oldest high-level language still in common use?
facts.explanation: John McCarthy began developing Lisp in 1958 at MIT to create an AI programming language for the IBM 704. Lisp (LISt Processing) pioneered many features including dynamic typing, garbage collection, and recursive functions. Modern dialects include Common Lisp, Scheme, Racket, and Clojure.
facts.visualDescription: A 1958 MIT computing lab with Lisp parentheses and linked list diagrams on a chalkboard, an IBM 704 mainframe in the background

facts.quizQuestion: What does LISP stand for, reflecting the language's primary data structure?
facts.explanation: LISP stands for LISt Processing, reflecting that its primary data structure is the linked list and that code itself is represented as lists. This property (code as data) is called 'homoiconicity' and is one of Lisp's most distinctive features. Lisp was invented by John McCarthy at MIT in 1958.
facts.visualDescription: A Lisp code snippet showing deeply nested parentheses, with a linked list of nodes drawn below illustrating the data structure
distractors: Language of Iterative and Sequential Processing

facts.quizQuestion: What does COBOL stand for?
facts.explanation: COBOL stands for Common Business-Oriented Language. Designed in 1959 by the CODASYL committee under US Department of Defense sponsorship, COBOL was heavily influenced by Grace Hopper's FLOW-MATIC. Despite its age, an estimated 800 billion lines of COBOL code remain in production in financial and mainframe systems.
facts.visualDescription: A 1960s business computing room with COBOL code printouts describing financial transactions in English-like prose

facts.quizQuestion: Whose FLOW-MATIC language directly influenced the design of COBOL — the language that still powers an estimated 800 billion lines of banking and mainframe code today?
facts.explanation: Grace Hopper's FLOW-MATIC was a key influence on COBOL's design. COBOL was designed in 1959 by the CODASYL committee as a US DoD initiative, partly based on FLOW-MATIC. Today an estimated 800 billion lines of COBOL remain in production in banks, insurance companies, and government systems worldwide.
facts.visualDescription: A modern bank data centre with COBOL source code on a monitor, and a 1959 Grace Hopper photo framed on the wall above it

facts.quizQuestion: Who co-created BASIC with Thomas Kurtz at Dartmouth College in 1964, designing it specifically so non-science students could write and use computer programs?
facts.explanation: John G. Kemeny and Thomas E. Kurtz created BASIC (Beginner's All-purpose Symbolic Instruction Code) at Dartmouth College in 1964. They designed it to be accessible to students in non-scientific fields. Microsoft was co-founded partly on Bill Gates and Paul Allen's BASIC interpreter for the Altair 8800 in 1975.
facts.visualDescription: A Dartmouth College computer terminal in 1964, a student typing 10 PRINT 'HELLO WORLD', the BASIC program listing appearing on paper tape

facts.quizQuestion: What language, created at Xerox PARC by Alan Kay, Dan Ingalls, and Adele Goldberg, pioneered object-oriented programming and also introduced the model-view-controller (MVC) architectural pattern?
facts.explanation: Smalltalk was created at Xerox PARC's Learning Research Group starting in 1970; a working interpreter was demonstrated in September 1972. It was one of the first purely object-oriented languages — everything is an object. It pioneered graphical user interfaces, the desktop metaphor, and the MVC pattern, heavily influencing Java, Python, Ruby, and most subsequent OO languages.
facts.visualDescription: A Xerox Alto computer at Xerox PARC in 1973, its bit-mapped display showing a graphical desktop with overlapping windows — all built in Smalltalk

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the C programming language at Bell Labs between 1969 and 1973, developing it alongside the Unix operating system?
facts.explanation: Dennis Ritchie created C at Bell Labs between 1969 and 1973, developing it alongside Unix. Unix was rewritten in C, making it portable across hardware — a revolutionary approach. C influenced the design of countless later languages including C++, Java, C#, Objective-C, JavaScript, Python, and Rust. The classic book 'The C Programming Language' (K&R C, 1978) remains influential.
facts.visualDescription: A 1973 Bell Labs terminal with C source code visible, a Unix manual beside it, both on a wooden desk in a dimly lit office

facts.quizQuestion: What was SQL originally called when Donald Chamberlin and Raymond Boyce developed it at IBM in the early 1970s, before a trademark conflict forced a name change?
facts.explanation: SQL was originally called SEQUEL (Structured English Query Language), developed at IBM's San Jose Research Laboratory by Donald Chamberlin and Raymond Boyce after learning about Edgar Codd's relational model. The name was shortened to SQL due to a trademark conflict with another product named SEQUEL. The first published description appeared in 1974.
facts.visualDescription: A 1974 IBM research paper with 'SEQUEL' on the cover, the letters 'U' and 'E' crossed out with an arrow pointing to 'SQL'

facts.quizQuestion: What was C++ originally called when Bjarne Stroustrup began developing it at Bell Labs in 1979?
facts.explanation: Bjarne Stroustrup began developing C++ at Bell Labs in 1979 as 'C with Classes' — an extension of C adding object-oriented features. It was renamed C++ (using the C increment operator to suggest an improved version of C) and first released externally in 1985. C++ became standardised as C++98 in 1997/1998.
facts.visualDescription: A 1979 Bell Labs whiteboard showing 'C with Classes' evolving via an arrow into 'C++', class hierarchy diagrams drawn beside it

facts.quizQuestion: Which company adopted Objective-C for its NeXTSTEP operating system and later made it the primary language for macOS and iOS — until replacing it with Swift in 2014?
facts.explanation: Objective-C was developed by Brad Cox and Tom Love in the early 1980s at Stepstone, adding Smalltalk-style message passing to C. Steve Jobs selected it for NeXT's operating system. When Apple acquired NeXT in 1997, Objective-C became the primary language for macOS (via Cocoa) and iOS (via Cocoa Touch), until Apple announced Swift in 2014.
facts.visualDescription: A NeXT computer workstation from 1988, then an iPhone from 2007, linked by Objective-C code snippets — the same language powering both

facts.quizQuestion: Who created Perl in 1987 as a Unix scripting language, with the design motto 'there is more than one way to do it'?
facts.explanation: Larry Wall developed Perl in 1987 as a general-purpose Unix scripting language for report processing. It became the dominant language for CGI web programming in the 1990s. Its design motto is TIMTOWTDI — 'There Is More Than One Way To Do It'. Despite 'Perl' not officially being an acronym, 'Practical Extraction and Reporting Language' is a common backronym.
facts.visualDescription: A Unix terminal in 1987 with a Perl script processing a text file, 'TIMTOWTDI' scrawled on a Post-it note on the monitor

acceptableAlternatives: Monty Python's Flying Circus (BBC comedy series)
facts.quizQuestion: Python is named after what BBC comedy television series — not the snake?
facts.explanation: Guido van Rossum named Python after Monty Python's Flying Circus, the BBC comedy series, not after the snake. Van Rossum was reading the published scripts from the show while developing the language and wanted a name that was short, unique, and slightly mysterious. Python 0.9.0 was released in 1991.
facts.visualDescription: A Python logo beside a still from Monty Python's Flying Circus, with a caption 'NOT named after this' showing a python snake with a red X
distractors: Monty Python and the Holy Grail

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Guido van Rossum release the first version of Python (0.9.0)?
facts.explanation: Guido van Rossum first released Python 0.9.0 in 1991, after beginning development in the late 1980s as a successor to the ABC language. Python emphasises code readability via significant indentation and became one of the most popular languages in the world, dominant in data science, AI/ML, and scripting.
facts.visualDescription: A 1991 Python 0.9.0 release announcement on a newsgroup terminal, the simple Python logo displayed beneath it

facts.quizQuestion: Who conceived the Ruby programming language in 1993 in Japan, publicly releasing it in December 1995 with the goal of making programming fun?
facts.explanation: Yukihiro 'Matz' Matsumoto conceived Ruby in 1993 and released Ruby 0.95 on Japanese domestic newsgroups on 21 December 1995. Everything in Ruby is an object, including primitive types. The Ruby on Rails framework (2004) made Ruby highly popular for web development.
facts.visualDescription: A Japanese developer in 1995 at a computer with Japanese newsgroup text on screen, the Ruby programming language name written in English on a notepad

facts.quizQuestion: Which language, created by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems in 1995, promised 'write once, run anywhere' by compiling to bytecode that runs on any Virtual Machine?
facts.explanation: Java, created by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems and publicly released in 1995, uses the 'write once, run anywhere' (WORA) model. Java source code compiles to platform-independent bytecode that runs on any Java Virtual Machine (JVM). It was originally called Oak, then Green, before the name Java (after Java coffee) was chosen.
facts.visualDescription: A 1995 Sun Microsystems advertisement showing the same Java program running on different computer platforms — Mac, PC, and Unix workstation — with 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' as the headline

facts.quizQuestion: Which language, despite its similar name, is completely unrelated to Java — originally called LiveScript and renamed purely for marketing reasons in 1995?
facts.explanation: JavaScript was created by Brendan Eich at Netscape in 1995. It was originally called LiveScript and renamed JavaScript to capitalise on Java's popularity at the time. Despite the similar name, JavaScript and Java are fundamentally different languages with very different designs. JavaScript is used by 98.9% of websites as of 2025.
facts.visualDescription: A split image: the Java logo (coffee cup) and the JavaScript logo (JS in a yellow square), connected by an equals-sign with a red X through it

facts.quizQuestion: What does PHP stand for today — a recursive acronym where the first letter stands for itself, a form of programmer humour?
facts.explanation: PHP now stands for 'PHP: Hypertext Preprocessor' — a recursive acronym where PHP stands for PHP. Originally it stood for 'Personal Home Page' when Rasmus Lerdorf created it in 1993. It powers a large portion of the web including WordPress, which runs approximately 43% of all websites.
facts.visualDescription: A PHP code snippet embedded in an HTML file, the text 'PHP: Hypertext Preprocessor' shown in neon letters recursively containing itself

facts.quizQuestion: Which American logician and mathematician is the Haskell programming language named after?
facts.explanation: Haskell is named after logician Haskell Curry, who made foundational contributions to combinatory logic and the lambda calculus. The first version, Haskell 1.0, was defined in 1990 by a committee that formed in 1987 to create an open standard for lazy functional languages. Features include type classes, monadic I/O, and lazy evaluation.
facts.visualDescription: A portrait of logician Haskell Curry beside a Haskell code snippet showing type signatures and function composition operators

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman first post a binary of the R statistical computing language — a language named partly from the shared first initial of its two creators?
facts.explanation: Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman posted a binary of R on StatLib in August 1993, having created it at the University of Auckland, New Zealand. R is named partly from the shared first initial of its authors (Ross and Robert) and as a reference to the S language it was inspired by. It became freely available under the GPL in December 1997.
facts.visualDescription: A University of Auckland statistics department computer lab in 1993, a terminal showing early R output with a statistical analysis plot

facts.quizQuestion: Which Unix co-creator was part of the Google team that designed the Go programming language in 2007, alongside Robert Griesemer and Rob Pike?
facts.explanation: Ken Thompson, who co-designed Unix at Bell Labs in 1969, co-designed Go at Google in 2007 with Robert Griesemer and Rob Pike. Go was publicly announced in November 2009. It features garbage collection, structural typing, and CSP-style concurrency via goroutines and channels. Docker and Kubernetes are written in Go.
facts.visualDescription: A Google whiteboard with 'Go language design' notes, three engineers sketching goroutine diagrams, the Go Gopher mascot doodled in the corner

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the Rust programming language in 2006, originally as a personal project while working at Mozilla, focusing on memory safety without a garbage collector?
facts.explanation: Graydon Hoare created Rust in 2006 while working at Mozilla as a personal project. Mozilla officially sponsored it in 2009, and Rust 1.0 was released in May 2015. Rust uses an ownership and borrowing system to guarantee memory safety without a garbage collector. It was the most-loved programming language in Stack Overflow's developer survey every year from 2016 to 2023.
facts.visualDescription: A Mozilla developer at a 2006 laptop with Rust code on screen, the Rust logo (an R inside a gear) visible on a sticker on the laptop lid

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the Swift programming language for Apple in 2010 — and also created the LLVM compiler infrastructure that Swift is built on?
facts.explanation: Chris Lattner created Swift for Apple in 2010, publicly released in June 2014. Lattner also created the LLVM compiler infrastructure project, which Swift's compiler is built on. Swift was designed to be safer, faster, and more modern than Objective-C, and Apple open-sourced it in December 2015.
facts.visualDescription: An Apple WWDC 2014 stage with 'Introducing Swift' on the screen, the Swift bird logo soaring above the podium

facts.quizQuestion: Who developed TypeScript at Microsoft, publicly releasing it in October 2012 — the same person who was lead architect of C# and creator of Delphi and Turbo Pascal?
facts.explanation: Anders Hejlsberg developed TypeScript at Microsoft after two years of internal development, publicly releasing version 0.8 in October 2012. Hejlsberg is also the lead architect of C# and the creator of Delphi and Turbo Pascal — making him one of the most influential language designers in history. TypeScript is a strict superset of JavaScript that transpiles to JavaScript.
facts.visualDescription: A Microsoft developer conference slide showing TypeScript as a superset of JavaScript, 'Anders Hejlsberg' credited as designer, a blue TS logo prominent

facts.quizQuestion: Which JetBrains language, first given stable release in February 2016, did Google make the preferred language for Android development in 2019?
facts.explanation: JetBrains unveiled Kotlin in July 2011; its first stable release (Kotlin 1.0) was on 15 February 2016. Google announced first-class Android support for Kotlin at Google I/O 2017 and made it the preferred language for Android in 2019. Kotlin is designed to interoperate fully with Java while fixing many of Java's pain points.
facts.visualDescription: An Android Studio IDE window with Kotlin code highlighted, a Google I/O 2019 badge reading 'Kotlin-first Android development' in the corner

facts.quizQuestion: Who first described HTML in 1991 in a document called 'HTML Tags', listing just 18 elements — the markup language that now defines every web page?
facts.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee first mentioned HTML (HyperText Markup Language) on the internet in late 1991 in a document called 'HTML Tags', describing just 18 elements. HTML defines the structure and content of web pages using elements and tags. The HTML 4.01 standard (1999) and HTML5 (2014) are the most significant later versions.
facts.visualDescription: A 1991 NeXT workstation display showing a simple HTML document with 18 tags listed, the first website structure ever described

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Håkon Wium Lie first propose Cascading Style Sheets while working with Tim Berners-Lee at CERN?
facts.explanation: CSS was first proposed by Håkon Wium Lie on 10 October 1994, while working with Tim Berners-Lee at CERN. Bert Bos's proposal was also influential; he became co-author of CSS1 and is regarded as co-creator of CSS. The first W3C CSS Recommendation (CSS1) was released in 1996.
facts.visualDescription: A CERN workstation in 1994 displaying an early webpage with unstyled text, a proposal document titled 'Cascading HTML Style Sheets' on the desk beside it

facts.quizQuestion: Which language family, first specified in 1958 by an international committee, introduced block structure (begin/end), lexical scoping, and is the ancestor of C, Pascal, Java, and most modern languages?
facts.explanation: ALGOL (Algorithmic Language) was first specified in 1958 by an international committee. ALGOL 60 (1960) was enormously influential, introducing block structure, lexical scoping, and nested functions — described using Backus-Naur Form (BNF). Most modern languages (C, Pascal, Java, etc.) are direct descendants of ALGOL's design.
facts.visualDescription: A 1960 ALGOL 60 report document open to its block structure syntax definition, with an evolutionary tree chart showing C, Pascal, Java all descending from ALGOL

facts.quizQuestion: Which 17th-century French mathematician and philosopher is the Pascal programming language (designed by Niklaus Wirth in 1968–1970) named after?
facts.explanation: Niklaus Wirth designed Pascal in 1968–1970, naming it after Blaise Pascal, the 17th-century French mathematician and philosopher who built one of the first mechanical calculators. Pascal became the dominant teaching language in university CS courses during the 1970s and 1980s. Apple's Lisa and early Macintosh were primarily programmed in Pascal.
facts.visualDescription: A portrait of 17th-century mathematician Blaise Pascal beside the Pascal language logo, connected by an arrow labelled 'named after'

facts.quizQuestion: Which first computer programmer is the Ada programming language — developed for the US Department of Defense in the late 1970s for safety-critical systems — named in honour of?
facts.explanation: Ada, designed in the late 1970s by a team led by Jean Ichbiah under US DoD contract, is named in honour of Ada Lovelace, the first computer programmer. The language was designed for safety-critical systems including aviation, military, and space applications, and is used in the Boeing 777, Airbus aircraft, and European Space Agency missions.
facts.visualDescription: A Boeing 777 cockpit display running avionics software, with an Ada source code snippet overlaid and the name 'Ada Lovelace' in the title bar

facts.quizQuestion: What does Prolog stand for — reflecting its logic-programming approach where you declare facts and rules and let the interpreter find solutions?
facts.explanation: Prolog stands for PROgramming in LOGic. Developed by Alain Colmerauer and Philippe Roussel at the Université de Marseille in 1972, Prolog programs declare facts and rules; the interpreter uses resolution to find solutions. It was heavily used in AI and computational linguistics, and Japan's Fifth Generation Computer project (1982–1992) chose Prolog as its core language.
facts.visualDescription: A Prolog inference engine diagram showing facts and rules at the top, an interpreter applying resolution, and a query answer at the bottom

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented Quicksort in 1959 while a visiting student in Moscow, making it one of the most widely used sorting algorithms ever?
facts.explanation: Tony Hoare conceived Quicksort in 1959 while at Moscow State University and published it in 1961. The algorithm uses divide-and-conquer: selecting a pivot, partitioning elements into smaller/larger groups, and recursing. Hoare later called his invention of the null reference a 'billion-dollar mistake'.
facts.visualDescription: Array of colored blocks being rapidly partitioned around a glowing pivot element by a recursive divide-and-conquer pattern

facts.quizQuestion: Merge Sort was invented in 1945 by a computing pioneer famous for the architecture inside nearly every modern computer. Who was it?
facts.explanation: John von Neumann invented Merge Sort in 1945. The algorithm uses divide-and-conquer: recursively splitting the array, sorting each half, then merging. It guarantees O(n log n) in all cases and is stable, making it a cornerstone sorting algorithm.
facts.visualDescription: A branching binary tree of array segments being split apart and then recombined in sorted order, like a symmetrical data waterfall

facts.quizQuestion: What Python core developer invented Timsort in 2002, a hybrid algorithm that became the default sort in Python and Java?
facts.explanation: Tim Peters created Timsort in 2002 for Python. It is a hybrid of Merge Sort and Insertion Sort that exploits natural 'runs' (already-sorted sequences) in real-world data. Python has used it as its default sort since version 2.3, and Java adopted it for object arrays in Java 7.
facts.visualDescription: The Python and Java logos flanking a sorted array where naturally-ordered 'runs' are highlighted in different colors before merging

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented Heapsort in 1964, publishing it in the Communications of the ACM and also introducing the binary heap data structure?
facts.explanation: J.W.J. Williams invented Heapsort in 1964 (Algorithm 232 in CACM). Robert W. Floyd refined the algorithm in the same year. Heapsort uses a binary heap to repeatedly extract the maximum element, achieving guaranteed O(n log n) in all cases with O(1) auxiliary space.
facts.visualDescription: A binary heap represented as a pyramid of numbered blocks, with the maximum element being repeatedly extracted from the top

facts.quizQuestion: What is the average-case time complexity of Bubble Sort, the 'simple but slow' algorithm that repeatedly swaps adjacent out-of-order elements?
facts.explanation: Bubble Sort has O(n²) average and worst-case complexity. It repeatedly compares and swaps adjacent elements, causing smaller values to 'bubble' upward. While easy to understand and implement, it is far too slow for large datasets and is primarily used for teaching.
facts.visualDescription: A row of numbered blocks with arrows showing adjacent swaps, smaller elements bubbling upward like carbonation

facts.quizQuestion: Merge Sort is one of the few sorting algorithms that guarantees the same time complexity in ALL cases — best, average, and worst. What is it?
facts.explanation: Merge Sort runs in O(n log n) in all cases — best, average, and worst. Unlike Quicksort, it never degrades to O(n²). This guarantee, combined with its stability, makes it the preferred choice for sorting linked lists and external (disk-based) sorting.
facts.visualDescription: Three identical complexity graphs — best, average, and worst — all showing the same n log n curve, highlighted green

facts.quizQuestion: Binary Search is dramatically faster than linear search on sorted arrays. What is its time complexity?
facts.explanation: Binary Search has O(log n) time complexity. By repeatedly halving the search interval, it finds a target in a sorted array far faster than scanning linearly. Searching one billion sorted items requires at most 30 comparisons. It requires the array to be sorted first.
facts.visualDescription: A sorted row of 1,024 blocks with a glowing cursor halving the search space repeatedly until zeroing in on the target

facts.quizQuestion: Quicksort is fast in practice but has a nasty worst case on already-sorted arrays with a poor pivot choice. What is that worst-case complexity?
facts.explanation: Quicksort's worst-case time complexity is O(n²), occurring when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (e.g. on an already-sorted array with naive pivot selection). In practice, randomized or median-of-three pivot selection avoids this. Average case remains O(n log n).
facts.visualDescription: A sorted array with the first element chosen as pivot each time, creating a deeply lopsided partition tree that degrades to linear scanning

facts.quizQuestion: Depth-First Search (DFS) explores as far as possible down each branch before backtracking. What data structure — or its implicit equivalent in recursion — powers this?
facts.explanation: DFS uses a stack (or the implicit call stack in recursion). It pushes nodes to visit and pops the most recently added, exploring deep paths before backtracking. DFS is used for topological sorting, cycle detection, maze-solving, and connected components detection.
facts.visualDescription: A graph with arrows plunging deep into one branch before backtracking, with a stack data structure on the side tracking the path

facts.quizQuestion: Breadth-First Search (BFS) finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph by exploring all neighbors level by level. What data structure makes this possible?
facts.explanation: BFS uses a queue (FIFO). Nodes to visit are added to the back and processed from the front, ensuring all vertices at distance k are explored before any at distance k+1. This guarantees the shortest path (fewest edges) is found first.
facts.visualDescription: A graph with nodes lit up level by level outward from the source like ripples in water, with a queue tracking the frontier

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was A* Search published by Hart, Nilsson, and Raphael at SRI International, giving game AI and GPS the most famous pathfinding algorithm ever?
facts.explanation: A* was published in 1968 by Peter Hart, Nils Nilsson, and Bertram Raphael at SRI International. It combines Dijkstra's actual-cost tracking with a heuristic estimate of remaining distance. A* is optimal when the heuristic never overestimates (admissible heuristic). It dominates game AI pathfinding to this day.
facts.visualDescription: A grid maze with a glowing optimal path highlighted, numbers showing the combined g+h cost estimate at each explored cell

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented the shortest-path algorithm in 1956 that powers GPS navigation and internet routing protocols to this day?
facts.explanation: Edsger W. Dijkstra conceived his shortest-path algorithm in 1956 and published it in 1959. He reportedly designed it in 20 minutes as a demonstration of his thinking. The algorithm finds the shortest path from a source to all other nodes in a weighted graph, but only works with non-negative edge weights.
facts.visualDescription: A weighted graph with glowing shortest-path lines radiating outward from a source node, distances labeled at each vertex

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Dijkstra publish his famous shortest-path algorithm in Numerische Mathematik?
facts.explanation: Dijkstra conceived his algorithm in 1956 but published it in 1959 in 'Numerische Mathematik' ('A note on two problems in connexion with graphs'). It remains one of the most cited algorithms in computer science.
facts.visualDescription: The cover of a 1959 mathematics journal with a hand-drawn graph sketched in the margins

facts.quizQuestion: Dijkstra's Algorithm fails on certain graph types, but the Bellman-Ford Algorithm handles them correctly. What does Bellman-Ford support that Dijkstra cannot?
facts.explanation: Bellman-Ford handles graphs with negative-weight edges. Dijkstra's greedy approach breaks when negative edges exist. Bellman-Ford is slower — O(V×E) vs Dijkstra's O((V+E) log V) — but can detect negative-weight cycles and is used in distance-vector routing protocols like RIP.
facts.visualDescription: Two graphs side by side: one with all positive weights where both algorithms succeed, and one with a negative-weight edge where only Bellman-Ford finds the correct path

facts.quizQuestion: Joseph Kruskal's 1956 algorithm sorts edges by weight and greedily adds them without forming cycles. What problem does it solve?
facts.explanation: Kruskal's Algorithm finds the minimum spanning tree (MST) of a weighted undirected graph — the set of edges that connects all vertices with the minimum total weight. It uses a Union-Find data structure for cycle detection. MSTs are used in network design, clustering, and approximation algorithms.
facts.visualDescription: A weighted undirected graph with the minimum spanning tree edges highlighted in green, while heavier redundant edges remain grey

facts.quizQuestion: The Floyd-Warshall Algorithm finds shortest paths between ALL pairs of vertices using dynamic programming. What is its time complexity?
facts.explanation: Floyd-Warshall runs in O(V³) time and O(V²) space. Published by Robert Floyd and Stephen Warshall in 1962, it uses dynamic programming to consider each vertex as a potential intermediate node. It handles negative-weight edges (but not negative cycles) and is well-suited for dense graphs.
facts.visualDescription: An adjacency matrix being iteratively updated with shortest-path distances as each vertex is considered as an intermediate node

facts.quizQuestion: A stack data structure follows what access pattern — the last element added is the first one removed?
facts.explanation: A stack uses LIFO (Last In, First Out) ordering. Core operations are push (add to top), pop (remove from top), and peek (view top without removing). Stacks power function call frames, undo operations, expression parsing, and Depth-First Search.
facts.visualDescription: A vertical stack of colored blocks with an arrow pushing one on top, and another arrow removing from the top only

facts.quizQuestion: A queue data structure — used in BFS and task scheduling — follows what access pattern?
facts.explanation: A queue uses FIFO (First In, First Out) ordering. Elements are enqueued at the rear and dequeued from the front. This mirrors a real-world queue of people. Queues are fundamental to BFS graph traversal, print spoolers, message passing systems, and CPU task scheduling.
facts.visualDescription: A horizontal row of colored blocks with arrows adding to the right end and removing from the left end, like people in a checkout line

facts.quizQuestion: Hash tables are used in Python dicts and JavaScript objects for a reason. What is the average-case time complexity for lookup, insertion, and deletion?
facts.explanation: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case time for lookup, insertion, and deletion. A hash function maps keys to array indices, enabling near-instant access. Worst case degrades to O(n) if many keys collide. Python's dict and JavaScript's object are both hash tables under the hood.
facts.visualDescription: Keys being fed through a hash function into colored buckets in an array, with arrows showing O(1) direct access to the correct bucket

facts.quizQuestion: A balanced Binary Search Tree (BST) stores values so that every left subtree is smaller and every right subtree is larger. What is its average-case search complexity?
facts.explanation: A balanced BST has O(log n) average-case search, insert, and delete. Each comparison halves the remaining search space. However, if the tree becomes unbalanced (degenerate), worst case degrades to O(n). Self-balancing variants like AVL and Red-Black trees guarantee O(log n) worst case.
facts.visualDescription: A symmetrical binary tree with a highlighted search path zigzagging down through left/right comparisons to find a target value

facts.quizQuestion: In 1936, who proved that no algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program will halt or run forever — a result that shook the foundations of mathematics?
facts.explanation: Alan Turing proved the undecidability of the halting problem in his 1936 paper 'On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem.' Using a clever diagonal argument, he showed that no general algorithm can decide whether any arbitrary program halts. This established fundamental limits on what computers can ever compute.
facts.visualDescription: A 1936-era Turing Machine diagram with an infinite tape, a question mark replacing the halting state — undecidable by any algorithm

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did both Alonzo Church (lambda calculus) and Alan Turing (Turing machines) independently publish their landmark models of computation?
facts.explanation: Both Alonzo Church and Alan Turing published their foundational models of computation in 1936. Church proposed lambda calculus; Turing proposed the abstract Turing machine. They independently arrived at the same conclusion — now called the Church-Turing thesis — that their models define exactly what is 'computable'.
facts.visualDescription: Two blackboards side by side: one covered in lambda calculus notation, the other showing a Turing machine diagram, both dated 1936

facts.quizQuestion: The P vs. NP problem is one of the greatest unsolved questions in computer science. How much does the Clay Mathematics Institute offer for its solution?
facts.explanation: The Clay Mathematics Institute offers $1,000,000 for a solution to P vs. NP — one of its seven Millennium Prize Problems.
