The question, formalized by Stephen Cook in 1971, asks whether every problem whose solution can be quickly verified can also be quickly solved. Most believe P ≠ NP, but neither direction has been proved.
facts.visualDescription: A chalkboard with 'P = NP ?' in enormous letters, with a million-dollar prize plaque mounted below it

facts.quizQuestion: Which 19th-century mathematician created the algebra of TRUE and FALSE values that underlies all digital logic and every computer ever built?
facts.explanation: George Boole created Boolean algebra in his 1847 work 'The Mathematical Analysis of Logic' and expanded it in 'The Laws of Thought' (1854). Claude Shannon showed in 1937 that Boolean algebra could describe electrical circuits, bridging abstract math and physical computing hardware.
facts.visualDescription: A Victorian-era mathematician's portrait beside a modern logic gate circuit diagram, connected by a two-sided arrow labeled 'AND, OR, NOT'

acceptableAlternatives: Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman
facts.quizQuestion: RSA encryption is named after its inventors' initials. Which three MIT researchers published the first practical public-key cryptosystem in 1977?
facts.explanation: RSA was published in 1977 by Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman at MIT — the acronym comes from their surnames. Its security rests on the difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers. Unknown to them, GCHQ's Clifford Cocks had discovered an equivalent algorithm in 1973, but it remained classified until 1997.
facts.visualDescription: Three MIT ID badges labeled R, S, and A beside a padlock icon and mathematical notation involving large prime numbers

facts.quizQuestion: PageRank is named after one of its creators. Which two Stanford PhD students invented it in 1998 and used it to build the world's dominant search engine?
facts.explanation: Larry Page and Sergey Brin developed PageRank at Stanford University in 1998 and used it as the foundation of Google's search engine. The algorithm ranks web pages by the number and quality of incoming links — a link from a highly-ranked page transfers more authority. The name comes from Larry Page, not 'web page'.
facts.visualDescription: A web graph with nodes of different sizes representing link authority, arrows flowing between them, the largest node labeled 'Google'

facts.quizQuestion: Which two Google engineers published the MapReduce paper in 2004, revealing how Google processed petabytes of data across thousands of machines?
facts.explanation: Jeffrey Dean and Sanjay Ghemawat published 'MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters' at OSDI 2004. The programming model splits work into Map (produce key-value pairs) and Reduce (aggregate) phases. Apache Hadoop (2006) brought the open-source implementation that powered much of the big data era.
facts.visualDescription: A distributed cluster of servers with Map workers processing chunks in parallel, then Reduce workers combining results into a final output

facts.quizQuestion: The Fast Fourier Transform, published by Cooley and Tukey in 1965, was revolutionary because it reduced DFT computation from O(n²) to what?
facts.explanation: The FFT reduces Discrete Fourier Transform computation from O(n²) to O(n log n) — a massive improvement for large inputs. Called one of the most important algorithms of the 20th century, it enables digital signal processing, JPEG image compression, MP3 audio, and telecommunications. Gauss had discovered equivalent methods around 1805 but never published them properly.
facts.visualDescription: A before/after complexity graph showing an O(n²) curve towering over an O(n log n) curve, with signal wave diagrams below

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams publish their landmark Nature paper popularizing Backpropagation for training neural networks?
facts.explanation: The landmark backpropagation paper by David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton, and Ronald Williams was published in Nature in 1986. This paper popularized the algorithm for training multi-layer neural networks. Earlier work by Paul Werbos (1974) and Linnainmaa (1970) had laid foundations, but the 1986 paper sparked widespread adoption.
facts.visualDescription: A multi-layer neural network diagram with error signals flowing backward through the layers as arrows, with 'Nature 1986' stamped across the top

facts.quizQuestion: The Cook-Levin Theorem (1971) proved the first NP-complete problem, opening the floodgates for Karp's 21 problems in 1972. What was that first NP-complete problem?
facts.explanation: Stephen Cook's 1971 theorem proved that Boolean satisfiability (SAT) is NP-complete — the first such proof. Richard Karp then showed 21 other classic problems (including Travelling Salesman, Graph Coloring, and Knapsack) reduce to NP-complete. If anyone solves any NP-complete problem in polynomial time, P = NP.
facts.visualDescription: A complex Boolean formula with AND/OR/NOT gates connected in a circuit, with a question mark asking whether any variable assignment satisfies it

facts.quizQuestion: Big-O notation is fundamental to algorithm analysis, but it was not invented by a computer scientist. Who introduced it in an 1894 mathematics book?
facts.explanation: Paul Bachmann introduced Big-O notation in his 1894 book 'Die Analytische Zahlentheorie.' Edmund Landau later popularized it — which is why it is also called 'Landau notation' or 'Bachmann-Landau notation.' Computer scientists adopted it to describe the upper-bound growth rate of algorithm running times.
facts.visualDescription: A late 19th-century German mathematics textbook open to a page introducing the 'O' symbol in formal analysis, with modern algorithm complexity charts overlaid

facts.quizQuestion: A binary heap (min-heap) always keeps the smallest element at its root. What is the time complexity of finding that minimum?
facts.explanation: Finding the minimum in a min-heap (or maximum in a max-heap) is O(1) because it is always stored at the root. Inserting and deleting are O(log n) as elements may need to bubble up or down. Heaps power priority queues, Dijkstra's algorithm, and A* search.
facts.visualDescription: A min-heap tree with the smallest value highlighted at the root, with O(1) labeled beside an arrow pointing directly to it

facts.quizQuestion: Which two Bell Labs engineers created Unix in 1969, writing the OS that would directly influence Linux, macOS, Android, and virtually every modern operating system?
facts.explanation: Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie created Unix in 1969 at Bell Labs (AT&T). It was originally written in assembly language for the PDP-7 and rewritten in C around 1973. Unix introduced foundational concepts like a hierarchical file system, pipes, the shell, and plain-text configuration that shaped all subsequent OS design.
facts.visualDescription: A 1969-era Bell Labs office with two engineers at a PDP-7 terminal, the word 'UNIX' scratched on a chalkboard behind them

facts.quizQuestion: Who was the 21-year-old Finnish university student who announced he was building a free hobby OS in August 1991, posting 'it won't be big and professional like gnu'?
facts.explanation: Linus Torvalds announced the Linux kernel on August 25, 1991, famously describing it as 'just a hobby, won't be big and professional like gnu' in a post to the comp.os.minix newsgroup. Version 0.01 followed on September 17. Today Linux powers 97% of the world's top 500 supercomputers and most web servers.
facts.visualDescription: A 1991-era university computer terminal showing the famous newsgroup post announcing Linux, a penguin mascot sketched in the corner

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Linus Torvalds announce the Linux kernel in his famous 'just a hobby' newsgroup post?
facts.explanation: Linus Torvalds posted his announcement to comp.os.minix on August 25, 1991. The first version (0.01) was released September 17, 1991. Version 1.0 followed in March 1994. Licensed under GNU GPL v2, Linux became the backbone of the open-source software world.
facts.visualDescription: A timeline of the early 1990s with 1991 highlighted, showing the Usenet post that started the Linux revolution

facts.quizQuestion: Who created CP/M in 1974 — the first widely-used OS for personal microcomputers — and is sometimes called 'the man who could have been Bill Gates'?
facts.explanation: Gary Kildall created CP/M (Control Program for Microcomputers) in 1974 for Intel 8080-based systems at Digital Research Inc. When IBM needed an OS for the PC, they approached Microsoft instead of Kildall. Microsoft licensed 86-DOS (essentially a CP/M clone) and delivered MS-DOS — a decision that made Bill Gates a billionaire.
facts.visualDescription: A vintage 1970s microcomputer running CP/M with a command prompt, and a 'what if' split-screen showing two diverging corporate histories

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was MS-DOS released as the operating system for the original IBM PC, beginning Microsoft's domination of personal computing?
facts.explanation: MS-DOS was released in 1981 as the OS for the original IBM PC. Microsoft acquired the underlying 86-DOS from Tim Paterson at Seattle Computer Products for around $50,000 and licensed it to IBM. This deal, not software brilliance, was the foundation of Microsoft's rise. MS-DOS dominated personal computing through the 1980s.
facts.visualDescription: An original IBM PC from 1981 with a blinking C:\ prompt on the screen, a floppy disk labeled 'MS-DOS 1.0' inserted

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Apple release the original Macintosh — the first commercially successful personal computer with a graphical user interface?
facts.explanation: The original Macintosh was released January 24, 1984, making it the first commercially successful personal computer with a GUI. The GUI concept came from Xerox PARC, which Steve Jobs visited in 1979. The Mac used a mouse-driven desktop with icons and windows. Modern macOS is built on Darwin (BSD Unix), a complete architectural rebuild starting with Mac OS X in 2001.
facts.visualDescription: The iconic original 1984 Macintosh with its small square screen showing a desktop, a mouse sitting beside it

facts.quizQuestion: Who founded Android Inc. in 2003 — the company Google acquired for ~$50 million in 2005 and turned into the world's most-used mobile operating system?
facts.explanation: Andy Rubin co-founded Android Inc. in 2003. Google acquired it in 2005 for approximately $50 million. The first commercial Android device was the HTC Dream (T-Mobile G1), released September 23, 2008. Android is based on the Linux kernel and has over 70% global smartphone market share.
facts.visualDescription: The original HTC Dream phone from 2008 with a keyboard sliding out, the Android robot mascot visible on the screen

facts.quizQuestion: Before Apple renamed it 'iOS' in 2010 when the iPad launched, what was Apple's mobile operating system originally called?
facts.explanation: Apple's mobile OS was originally called 'iPhone OS' when it launched with the first iPhone on June 29, 2007. It was renamed 'iOS' in 2010 when the iPad was released, as the name needed to cover more than just phones. iOS is based on Darwin (BSD Unix), the same foundation as macOS.
facts.visualDescription: The original iPhone from 2007 showing the classic iOS 1.0 interface, with 'iPhone OS' labeled below and an arrow transforming to 'iOS' in 2010

facts.quizQuestion: Often called the 'two fathers of the internet,' which duo published the TCP/IP paper in 1974 that defined how all internet communications work?
facts.explanation: Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn published 'A Protocol for Packet Network Intercommunication' in 1974, defining the TCP/IP protocol suite. ARPANET formally switched to TCP/IP on January 1, 1983 — often called the 'birthday of the internet.' Cerf and Kahn received the ACM Turing Award in 2004 for this work.
facts.visualDescription: Two engineers at 1974 computer terminals with diagrams of packet-switching networks, labeled 'Cerf' and 'Kahn', handshaking over a network diagram

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented Ethernet in 1973 at Xerox PARC, went on to co-found 3Com, and named his invention after the 19th-century concept of a light-carrying medium?
facts.explanation: Robert Metcalfe (with David Boggs) invented Ethernet at Xerox PARC in 1973. Metcalfe's design memo is dated May 22, 1973. The name 'Ethernet' comes from 'luminiferous ether' — the now-discredited 19th-century concept of the medium carrying light. Metcalfe co-founded 3Com in 1979 to commercialize Ethernet.
facts.visualDescription: A 1973 Xerox PARC memo sketch showing the original Ethernet bus topology, with the word 'Ether-net' written in architect's lettering

facts.quizQuestion: Packet switching was independently developed by Paul Baran (1964) and a British physicist in 1965. Who coined the actual term 'packet' that we still use today?
facts.explanation: Donald Davies at the UK National Physical Laboratory coined the term 'packet' in 1965. Paul Baran at RAND Corporation had independently developed the concept of breaking data into blocks in 1964. Together their work formed the theoretical foundation of ARPANET and the modern internet.
facts.visualDescription: A data stream being broken into labeled 'packets' numbered 1-5, each taking different routes across a network map before reassembling at the destination

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the Domain Name System (DNS) in 1983 — the 'phone book of the internet' that translates domain names into IP addresses?
facts.explanation: Paul Mockapetris designed DNS in 1983, defining it in RFC 882 and RFC 883. DNS translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) into machine-readable IP addresses. It is a hierarchical distributed database — not a single central lookup — organized into root servers, TLD servers (.com, .org), and authoritative servers.
facts.visualDescription: A browser address bar with 'google.com' being resolved through a DNS hierarchy of servers into a numeric IP address

facts.quizQuestion: Who created HTTP at CERN in 1991 as part of the World Wide Web, giving the internet the protocol that powers every webpage you load?
facts.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee created HTTP at CERN in 1991 as a core component of the World Wide Web. HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the application-layer protocol for transmitting HTML documents and other hypermedia. HTTP/0.9 was the first version; HTTP/1.1 became the standard by 1997. HTTPS adds TLS encryption.
facts.visualDescription: CERN's campus in 1991 with a computer screen showing the world's first webpage, the HTTP request/response cycle diagrammed above

facts.quizQuestion: Wi-Fi is a brand name — what is the underlying technical standard ratified in 1997 that all Wi-Fi devices must comply with?
facts.explanation: Wi-Fi is based on IEEE 802.11, first ratified in 1997. The Wi-Fi Alliance was formed in 1999 and branded the technology 'Wi-Fi.' Despite common belief, Wi-Fi does NOT stand for 'Wireless Fidelity' — it is a marketing term. The original standard offered 2 Mbit/s; modern Wi-Fi 6E (802.11ax) reaches ~9.6 Gbit/s.
facts.visualDescription: A wireless router with the Wi-Fi logo and beneath it the technical label 'IEEE 802.11', signal waves radiating outward

facts.quizQuestion: IPv4 addresses have a fixed bit length that limits the internet to roughly 4.3 billion unique addresses — how many bits is an IPv4 address?
facts.explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits long (defined in RFC 791, 1981), giving 2³² = approximately 4.3 billion possible addresses. This pool was exhausted by IANA in February 2011. IPv6 addresses are 128 bits, providing ~340 undecillion addresses. IPv5 was an experimental streaming protocol that was never publicly deployed.
facts.visualDescription: A dotted-decimal IPv4 address (e.g. 192.168.1.1) with its 32-bit binary representation below, contrasted with a longer 128-bit IPv6 address

facts.quizQuestion: Which British mathematician at Bletchley Park developed the Bombe machine to break Nazi Germany's Enigma cipher at scale during WWII?
facts.explanation: Alan Turing (with Gordon Welchman) developed the Bombe at Bletchley Park to crack Enigma messages at scale. Polish mathematicians had first broken Enigma in 1932, but Turing's improvements enabled systematic decryption. Breaking Enigma is estimated to have shortened WWII by 2-4 years. Turing was later prosecuted for homosexuality and died in 1954.
facts.visualDescription: The Bombe machine — a large cabinet of spinning rotors — at Bletchley Park, with intercepted Enigma messages stacked beside it

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman publish 'New Directions in Cryptography,' introducing the first publicly known public-key cryptography protocol?
facts.explanation: Diffie and Hellman published 'New Directions in Cryptography' in 1976 — the founding paper of public-key cryptography. Their key exchange allows two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel without prior contact. GCHQ's Malcolm Williamson had independently discovered it in 1974, but that remained classified until 1997. They received the Turing Award in 2015.
facts.visualDescription: Two people exchanging colored paint mixtures in a public demonstration of the Diffie-Hellman key exchange, arriving at the same secret color without revealing it

facts.quizQuestion: Who created PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) in 1991 and was investigated by the US government for 3 years for exporting strong encryption as free software?
facts.explanation: Phil Zimmermann released PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) in 1991 as free software for email encryption. The US government investigated him from 1993-1996 for violating export restrictions on cryptography — treating encryption software as munitions. The case was dropped in 1996. The OpenPGP standard (RFC 4880) is its modern open version.
facts.visualDescription: A 1991 computer screen showing PGP encryption in action with public/private keys, with a US government 'MUNITIONS' warning stamped across it

facts.quizQuestion: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) — the predecessor to TLS that secures HTTPS today — was created by which browser company in 1995?
facts.explanation: SSL was created by Netscape Communications. SSL 2.0 was released in 1995 as part of Netscape Navigator to secure e-commerce transactions. TLS (Transport Layer Security) replaced SSL; TLS 1.0 was defined in RFC 2246 (1999). The current standard is TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018). HTTPS = HTTP over TLS.
facts.visualDescription: A 1995 Netscape Navigator browser window with the padlock icon in the corner showing a secure HTTPS connection, the SSL handshake diagrammed above

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Morris Worm — the first internet worm to gain widespread attention — infect ~10% of all machines on the internet?
facts.explanation: The Morris Worm was released November 2, 1988 by Cornell University graduate student Robert Tappan Morris. It infected approximately 6,000 machines (~10% of the internet at the time), exploiting vulnerabilities in Unix sendmail, fingerd, and rsh/rexec. Morris was the first person convicted under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act. The incident led directly to the creation of CERT.
facts.visualDescription: A 1988 network map of the early internet with infected nodes lighting up like a spreading fire, a Cornell University banner in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who released the first internet worm in 1988, became the first person convicted under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, and later co-founded Y Combinator?
facts.explanation: Robert Tappan Morris released the Morris Worm on November 2, 1988 while a Cornell University graduate student. He became the first person convicted under the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (1986). Later in life, Morris co-founded Viaweb (acquired by Yahoo!) and was a co-founder of Y Combinator, the startup accelerator.
facts.visualDescription: A split biography image: left shows a 1988 Cornell student at a terminal with court documents, right shows a Y Combinator logo

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto publish the Bitcoin whitepaper, introducing blockchain and decentralized digital currency to the world?
facts.explanation: The Bitcoin whitepaper 'Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System' was published on October 31, 2008 by Satoshi Nakamoto — a pseudonym whose real identity remains unknown. The genesis block (Block 0) was mined January 3, 2009. Bitcoin introduced blockchain: a distributed, append-only, cryptographically-linked ledger.
facts.visualDescription: A Halloween-dated 2008 document titled 'Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System' with a mysterious signature 'Satoshi Nakamoto'

facts.quizQuestion: What does the acronym TCP stand for — the connection-oriented protocol that ensures reliable, ordered delivery of data across the internet?
facts.explanation: TCP stands for Transmission Control Protocol. Together with IP (Internet Protocol), it forms TCP/IP — the foundational protocol suite of the internet. TCP provides reliable, ordered, error-checked delivery by breaking data into segments, numbering them, and retransmitting lost ones. Defined in RFC 793 (1981).
facts.visualDescription: A diagram of a TCP connection handshake — SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK — with the letters T, C, P expanding into their full words below

facts.quizQuestion: What does UDP stand for — the fast but fire-and-forget protocol used for video streaming, DNS, and online gaming where speed beats reliability?
facts.explanation: UDP stands for User Datagram Protocol (RFC 768, 1980). Unlike TCP, UDP sends packets without establishing a connection or guaranteeing delivery, order, or error correction. This makes it much faster — ideal for DNS queries, video streaming, VoIP, and online gaming where occasional packet loss is acceptable but latency is critical.
facts.visualDescription: Packets being fired off like arrows with no acknowledgment, contrasted with TCP's careful handshake — both labeled with their speeds and trade-offs

facts.quizQuestion: What does FTP stand for — the 1971 standard protocol for transferring files between a client and a server over a network?
facts.explanation: FTP stands for File Transfer Protocol. Defined in RFC 959 (1985), FTP is one of the oldest internet protocols, standardized in 1971. It allows uploading and downloading files between client and server. Because it transmits credentials in plaintext, SFTP (SSH-secured) and FTPS (TLS-secured) are now preferred for security.
facts.visualDescription: A file directory tree on the left (local) with arrows moving files to a server directory tree on the right, labeled 'FTP'

facts.quizQuestion: What does SSH stand for — the 1995 protocol that replaced Telnet for secure remote login and command execution over an insecure network?
facts.explanation: SSH stands for Secure Shell. Created in 1995 by Tatu Ylönen at Helsinki University of Technology after a password-sniffing attack on his university network, SSH encrypts all communications. It replaced insecure protocols like Telnet and rsh. SSH runs on port 22 by default and is the standard tool for remote server administration.
facts.visualDescription: A terminal window showing an SSH login session to a remote server, the connection marked as encrypted with a padlock icon

facts.quizQuestion: What does SMTP stand for — the 1982 protocol that routes every email you send from server to server across the internet?
facts.explanation: SMTP stands for Simple Mail Transfer Protocol (RFC 821, 1982). It is the standard protocol for sending email between mail servers and from email clients to outgoing mail servers. SMTP handles sending only; POP3 and IMAP handle receiving. SMTP servers typically use port 25 (server-to-server) or port 587 (client-to-server).
facts.visualDescription: An email journey diagram: a user's laptop → SMTP server → internet cloud → recipient's SMTP server → inbox, each step labeled

facts.quizQuestion: What does DNS stand for — the 'phone book of the internet' that translates google.com into an IP address your computer can route to?
facts.explanation: DNS stands for Domain Name System. Created by Paul Mockapetris in 1983, it is a hierarchical distributed database that maps human-readable domain names to IP addresses. Without DNS, you would need to remember numeric IP addresses for every website. DNS queries are typically sent over UDP port 53.
facts.visualDescription: A browser's address bar with 'wikipedia.org' transforming into an IP address '208.80.154.224' via a DNS lookup chain of servers

facts.quizQuestion: What does RAM stand for — the volatile that temporarily stores your running programs and loses all data when power is removed?
facts.explanation: RAM stands for Random Access Memory. It is volatile (loses data when power is removed) and stores the data and program instructions currently in use by the CPU. 'Random access' means any memory location can be read at the same speed, unlike sequential-access storage. DRAM (Dynamic RAM) is the most common type in computers.
facts.visualDescription: A RAM stick with lightning bolt symbols showing fast read/write access to any cell, and a power plug being pulled with data disappearing

facts.quizQuestion: What does CPU stand for — the primary component of a computer that executes program instructions and is often called the 'brain' of the computer?
facts.explanation: CPU stands for Central Processing Unit. It fetches, decodes, and executes program instructions. Modern CPUs have multiple cores (enabling parallel execution) and include caches to minimize memory access latency. The Intel 4004 (1971) was the first single-chip microprocessor and pioneered the era of programmable CPUs.
facts.visualDescription: A modern CPU chip with multiple cores labeled, heat spreader on top, socket pins below, arrows showing the fetch-decode-execute cycle

facts.quizQuestion: What does GPU stand for — the term coined by NVIDIA in 1999 for a massively parallel processor originally designed for rendering graphics but now powering AI?
facts.explanation: GPU stands for Graphics Processing Unit, a term coined by NVIDIA in 1999 for the GeForce 256. GPUs have thousands of smaller cores optimized for parallel computation. Originally designed for rendering 3D graphics in video games, GPUs became the dominant hardware for training AI/ML models due to their massive parallel throughput.
facts.visualDescription: A GPU board with thousands of tiny CUDA cores visualized as a grid, running both a video game render and a neural network training task simultaneously

facts.correctAnswer: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
facts.quizQuestion: What does ASCII stand for — the 1963 7-bit character encoding standard that assigns numeric codes to letters, digits, and symbols, and is the basis for Unicode?
facts.explanation: ASCII stands for American Standard Code for Information Interchange, published in 1963 as ASA X3.4-1963. It uses 7-bit encoding to represent 128 characters: 33 control characters and 95 printable characters (letters, digits, punctuation). Unicode is a superset of ASCII, maintaining the same code points for the original 128 characters.
facts.visualDescription: An ASCII table showing letters A-Z with their numeric codes 65-90, and a Unicode table beside it showing the vastly expanded character set built on top
distractors: Automated Standard Coding for Information Interchange
American Symbol Code for Information Interchange
Advanced Standard Code for Information Interchange
ASCII Standard Coding for Internal Interchange
Automated Symbolic Code for Internal Interface
American Sequential Code for Information Interchange
Applied Standard Code for Information Interchange

facts.quizQuestion: What does USB stand for — the 1996 interface standard invented by Intel's Ajay Bhatt that replaced a jungle of connectors and now charges your phone?
facts.explanation: USB stands for Universal Serial Bus. USB 1.0 was released in January 1996, developed by a consortium led by Intel engineer Ajay Bhatt. USB replaced the chaos of serial ports, parallel ports, PS/2, and proprietary connectors with a single universal standard. Modern USB-C also delivers up to 240W of charging power.
facts.visualDescription: A mess of 1990s PC connectors (serial, parallel, PS/2) on the left, replaced by a single clean USB-C port on the right

facts.quizQuestion: What does BIOS stand for — the firmware Gary Kildall named in 1975 that initializes your PC hardware at boot before handing off to the operating system?
facts.explanation: BIOS stands for Basic Input/Output System, a term coined by Gary Kildall in CP/M in 1975. It is firmware stored on a motherboard chip that runs first when a computer powers on, initializing hardware (RAM, storage, peripherals) and loading the OS bootloader. BIOS has been largely replaced by UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface) in modern computers.
facts.visualDescription: A PC boot sequence diagram: power button → BIOS POST screen with hardware checks → OS loading screen, with the BIOS chip highlighted on the motherboard

facts.quizQuestion: What does LAN stand for — the type of the interconnected system that connects your home or office devices, typically using Ethernet cables or Wi-Fi?
facts.explanation: LAN stands for Local Area Network. A LAN connects computers and devices within a limited geographic area (a home, office, or building). Ethernet and Wi-Fi are the dominant LAN technologies. The internet itself is the largest WAN (Wide Area Network), which interconnects millions of LANs worldwide.
facts.visualDescription: A home office floor plan with computers, a printer, and a router all connected within the building, labeled 'LAN', with an arrow pointing outward labeled 'WAN = Internet'

facts.quizQuestion: What was the first message ever sent over the ARPANET on October 29, 1969 — before the receiving computer crashed?
facts.explanation: UCLA student programmer Charley Kline was attempting to type "login" to connect to Stanford Research Institute. He typed "l" and "o" before the SRI host crashed, making "lo" the accidental first message on the network that would become the internet.
facts.visualDescription: A 1960s computer terminal in a dim university lab, a student hunched over a keyboard, a blinking cursor on a green-phosphor screen

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the first message sent over the ARPANET, marking the birth of the internet?
facts.explanation: On October 29, 1969, the first host-to-host message was sent over ARPANET, connecting UCLA and Stanford Research Institute. The network that would eventually evolve into the internet had been born.
facts.visualDescription: A vintage 1960s mainframe room with tape reels spinning and blinking lights, two universities connected by a glowing line on a map

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented email in 1971 and chose the @ symbol to separate username from host — a convention we still use today?
facts.explanation: Ray Tomlinson, an American computer programmer, sent the first network email on the ARPANET in 1971. He selected the @ symbol because it appeared on keyboards and would never appear in a person's name, creating the universal address format still used today.
facts.visualDescription: A programmer at a 1970s teletype terminal, a stylized @ symbol floating above the machine in neon glow

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Ray Tomlinson send the first network email over ARPANET?
facts.explanation: Ray Tomlinson sent the first email between two ARPANET computers in 1971. He later admitted he couldn't remember what the first message said — it was likely a test string like 'QWERTYUIOP'.
facts.visualDescription: An early ARPANET terminal with a blinking cursor, the @ symbol glowing on screen, connected nodes visualized as dots on a network diagram

facts.quizQuestion: Who designed the Domain Name System (DNS) in 1983, giving us the ability to use names like 'example.com' instead of raw IP addresses?
facts.explanation: Paul Mockapetris designed the Domain Name System and published it in November 1983 as RFCs 882 and 883. DNS translates human-readable domain names into the numerical IP addresses computers use to find each other.
facts.visualDescription: A hierarchical tree diagram of domain names branching from a root node, glowing on a blueprint-style dark background

facts.quizQuestion: What was the very first .com domain name ever registered, on March 15, 1985?
facts.explanation: symbolics.com, registered by computer manufacturer Symbolics Inc. on March 15, 1985, was the first .com domain name in the world. The domain still exists today as a historical landmark, purchased by a domain investor in 2009.
facts.visualDescription: A vintage 1985 computer screen showing a blinking cursor next to 'symbolics.com', surrounded by the glow of early-era monitor phosphors

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at CERN, and also created HTML, HTTP, and URLs?
facts.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee, a British computer scientist at CERN, proposed the World Wide Web in 1989 as a system for sharing information using hypertext. He invented all three core technologies: HTML (the document format), HTTP (the transfer protocol), and URLs (the addressing system).
facts.visualDescription: A British scientist at a NeXT workstation in a Swiss research lab, a handwritten proposal reading 'Information Management: A Proposal' on the desk

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Tim Berners-Lee write his proposal for the World Wide Web at CERN?
facts.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee wrote 'Information Management: A Proposal' at CERN in 1989. His supervisor noted on it: 'Vague but exciting.' Three years later, in 1991, the first website went live.
facts.visualDescription: A paper document titled 'Information Management: A Proposal' with a handwritten note, resting on a desk at CERN with particle physics diagrams nearby

facts.quizQuestion: What was the URL of the world's first website, created by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN and launched in 1991?
facts.explanation: The world's first website at info.cern.ch went live in 1991. It described the World Wide Web project and explained how to use hypertext. The page is still accessible today at its original address, preserved by CERN as a historical artifact.
facts.visualDescription: An early 1990s web browser showing a simple text-heavy page at info.cern.ch, with blue hyperlinks on a white background

facts.quizQuestion: What 1993 browser was the first to display images inline with text on web pages, making the web visual and helping it go mainstream?
facts.explanation: NCSA Mosaic, released January 23, 1993, was the first browser to show images alongside text rather than in separate windows. Created by Marc Andreessen and Eric Bina at the National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA), it made the web accessible to non-technical users.
facts.visualDescription: A 1993 computer screen showing a colorful web page with inline photos, a browser window titled 'NCSA Mosaic', a university computing center in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who co-created the Mosaic web browser in 1993 and went on to co-found Netscape Communications?
facts.explanation: Marc Andreessen co-created NCSA Mosaic with Eric Bina at the National Center for Supercomputing Applications while a student at the University of Illinois. He later co-founded Netscape and is now a prominent venture capitalist at Andreessen Horowitz.
facts.visualDescription: A young programmer in a 1990s university computing lab, stacks of computer manuals nearby, a web browser prototype on the screen

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Netscape Navigator released, quickly becoming the dominant web browser of the 1990s?
facts.explanation: Netscape Navigator was first released on December 15, 1994. At its peak, Netscape held more than 90% of the browser market. The 'browser wars' with Microsoft's Internet Explorer began shortly after, and the competition drove rapid advances in web technology.
facts.visualDescription: A mid-1990s home computer showing the Netscape Navigator interface with its ship's wheel logo, an excited user browsing colorful early web pages

facts.quizQuestion: With which Microsoft operating system was Internet Explorer first bundled in 1995, triggering the browser wars?
facts.explanation: Internet Explorer was released on August 24, 1995, initially bundled with the Windows 95 Plus! add-on pack. Microsoft's decision to bundle IE directly with the Windows operating system was a central issue in the US vs. Microsoft antitrust case.
facts.visualDescription: A 1995 PC box for Windows 95, an 'e' Internet Explorer icon visible on the Start menu on screen, a crowded electronics store in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who popularized the term 'Web 2.0' around 2004, referring to the new era of user-generated content and participatory websites?
facts.explanation: Tim O'Reilly, founder of O'Reilly Media, popularized the concept of 'Web 2.0' around 2004, most notably through the O'Reilly Media Web 2.0 Conference. The term described a shift toward sites like Wikipedia, YouTube, and social networks where users created the content.
facts.visualDescription: A 2004 tech conference stage with a presenter gesturing at a slide reading 'Web 2.0', surrounded by eager early-2000s laptop users in the audience

facts.quizQuestion: Who created Archie in 1990 — considered the world's first internet search engine — while a postgraduate student at McGill University?
facts.explanation: Alan Emtage, a student from Barbados studying at McGill University in Montreal, created Archie in 1990 to index FTP archives so users could find specific files. Its name was simply 'archive' shortened to 'Archie' — not named after the comic book character.
facts.visualDescription: A 1990 university terminal showing a text-based search interface labeled 'Archie', a student peering at the green-screen results list

facts.quizQuestion: Which two Stanford PhD students founded Yahoo in January 1994, initially calling it 'the concept and David's Guide to the World Wide Web'?
facts.explanation: Jerry Yang and David Filo started Yahoo as a personal directory of websites they found interesting. The name Yahoo stood for 'Yet Another Hierarchically Organized Oracle' — though Yang and Filo claimed to like the word because it meant 'rude, unsophisticated' in Gulliver's Travels.
facts.visualDescription: Two graduate students at Stanford in a cramped trailer office, monitors showing a web directory with hand-curated site links

facts.quizQuestion: AltaVista, one of the most popular early search engines launched in 1995, was developed at which now-defunct computer company?
facts.explanation: AltaVista was developed at Digital Equipment Corporation (DEC) and launched December 15, 1995. It was famous for being among the first engines to index the full text of web pages. DEC was acquired by Compaq in 1998, and AltaVista was eventually bought by Yahoo in 2003.
facts.visualDescription: A 1995 workstation screen showing the AltaVista search interface — a bold logo, a search box, with early '90s corporate office surroundings

facts.quizQuestion: Which two Stanford PhD students incorporated Google on September 4, 1998, introducing the PageRank algorithm?
facts.explanation: Larry Page and Sergey Brin met as Stanford PhD students and developed Google's PageRank algorithm, which ranked pages by the number and quality of links pointing to them. They initially tried to sell the technology for $1 million but were rejected, so they built Google into the world's most-used search engine.
facts.visualDescription: Two graduate students in a Palo Alto garage, a whiteboard covered in math behind them, a monitor showing early Google search results

facts.quizQuestion: What was the name of the link-analysis algorithm developed by Larry Page that powered Google's early search superiority?
facts.explanation: PageRank (named after Larry Page, not 'page rank') ranked web pages by counting the number and quality of links pointing to them — the idea being that more important pages receive more links. This approach produced dramatically better search results than keyword-matching competitors.
facts.visualDescription: A network graph with nodes of varying sizes representing web pages, arrows showing link connections, larger nodes glowing brighter

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Wikipedia founded by Jimmy Wales and Larry Sanger, launching the world's largest collaboratively written encyclopedia?
facts.explanation: Wikipedia was founded on January 15, 2001, by Jimmy Wales and Larry Sanger. Starting with just a handful of articles, it grew to become the largest reference work in human history, with over 60 million articles across hundreds of languages maintained by volunteer editors.
facts.visualDescription: A puzzle-globe logo assembling itself from interlocking pieces in various scripts, floating above a glowing laptop screen

facts.quizQuestion: Who co-founded Wikipedia in 2001 — the free online encyclopedia that anyone can edit?
facts.explanation: Jimmy Wales and Larry Sanger co-founded Wikipedia on January 15, 2001. Sanger coined the name (combining 'wiki' — Hawaiian for 'quick' — and 'encyclopedia'). Wales and Sanger later had a dispute over credit for the founding and both have claimed primary credit.
facts.visualDescription: Two collaborators at a computer in an early 2000s home office, a mostly blank wiki page on screen, one of them typing the first article

facts.quizQuestion: Which Harvard student founded Facebook in 2004, initially restricting it to Harvard before opening it to universities worldwide?
facts.explanation: Mark Zuckerberg founded Facebook in February 2004 with his Harvard roommates Eduardo Saverin, Andrew McCollum, Dustin Moskovitz, and Chris Hughes. It launched as 'TheFacebook.com' for Harvard students before rapidly expanding. Zuckerberg dropped out of Harvard to run the company.
facts.visualDescription: A young man in a Harvard dorm room at a laptop, a social network profile visible on screen, late-night energy drinks on the desk

facts.quizQuestion: Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim — the three co-founders of YouTube — all previously worked together at what company?
facts.explanation: All three YouTube founders were former employees of PayPal, which had recently been acquired by eBay. They founded YouTube on Valentine's Day 2005. Many PayPal alumni went on to found major Silicon Valley companies — they were dubbed the 'PayPal Mafia.'
facts.visualDescription: Three young engineers in a San Bruno startup office, whiteboards full of video player sketches, early YouTube logo mockups pinned to the wall

facts.quizQuestion: Just 19 months after YouTube launched, which company acquired it in October 2006 for $1.65 billion?
facts.explanation: Google acquired YouTube in October 2006 for $1.65 billion in stock, one of the largest internet acquisitions at the time. The deal gave Google a dominant position in online video that persists to this day. YouTube is now estimated to be worth over $300 billion.
facts.visualDescription: Google and YouTube logos facing each other over a handshake, stacks of stock certificates in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who sent the very first tweet on March 21, 2006, posting 'just setting up my twttr' on the platform he co-founded?
facts.explanation: Jack Dorsey sent the first tweet on March 21, 2006, which read 'just setting up my twttr' — notice the original name had no vowels. Dorsey co-founded Twitter with Biz Stone, Noah Glass, and Evan Williams. He later co-founded Square (now Block) and returned to Twitter as CEO.
facts.visualDescription: A 2006 flip-phone and early desktop computer side by side, a tweet reading 'just setting up my twttr' glowing on both screens

facts.quizQuestion: Which two University of Virginia roommates founded Reddit in 2005, creating the platform known as 'the front page of the internet'?
facts.explanation: Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian founded Reddit in 2005 after attending a talk by Y Combinator founder Paul Graham. Reddit's upvote/downvote system and 'subreddit' communities became highly influential in internet culture.
facts.visualDescription: Two college roommates at a dorm desk with a laptop showing an early Reddit prototype, forum threads and upvote arrows sketched on a whiteboard

facts.quizQuestion: Who created Instagram, the photo-sharing app released in October 2010 that was acquired by Facebook for ~$1 billion just 18 months later?
facts.explanation: Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger created Instagram, which launched on October 12, 2010. Within 24 hours it had 25,000 users. In April 2012 Facebook acquired it for approximately $1 billion — widely considered one of the best acquisitions in tech history given Instagram's later valuation of over $100 billion.
facts.visualDescription: A 2010 iPhone screen showing the early Instagram app with a polaroid-style frame around a coffee photo, a startup office in San Francisco in the background

facts.quizQuestion: When Jeff Bezos founded Amazon in 1994, what single product category did it initially sell before becoming 'The Everything Store'?
facts.explanation: Jeff Bezos founded Amazon as an online bookstore because books had a massive catalog (millions of titles existed) and could be easily shipped. He chose the name 'Amazon' after the world's largest river to suggest scale. The company only began selling other products in 1998.
facts.visualDescription: A 1995 home computer screen showing an early Amazon website listing book titles, stacks of books in a suburban garage warehouse in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Who founded eBay in September 1995, pioneering the online auction model with an early listing for a broken laser pointer?
facts.explanation: Pierre Omidyar founded eBay as 'AuctionWeb' in San Jose, California in September 1995. Legend has it the first item sold was a broken laser pointer for $14.83. Omidyar contacted the buyer to confirm they understood it was broken — the buyer confirmed they collected broken laser pointers.
facts.visualDescription: A 1995 home computer screen showing an early auction listing with a bid counter ticking up, a vintage colorful eBay logo in the corner

facts.quizQuestion: PayPal was founded in December 1998 — but under what original name, founded by Peter Thiel and Max Levchin?
facts.explanation: PayPal was originally founded as Confinity by Peter Thiel, Max Levchin, and others in December 1998. It later merged with Elon Musk's X.com in 2000 and was renamed PayPal. The company was acquired by eBay in 2002 for $1.5 billion. Former PayPal employees went on to found YouTube, LinkedIn, Yelp, and other major companies.
facts.visualDescription: A late-1990s startup office with the word 'Confinity' on the window, a whiteboard sketching a digital wallet concept

facts.quizQuestion: Netflix was founded in 1997 as a DVD-by-mail service. In what year did it launch streaming, launching the era of binge-watching?
facts.explanation: Netflix launched its video streaming service in 2007, a decade after founding as a DVD-by-mail service. The streaming launch eventually upended the cable TV industry and prompted major Hollywood studios to launch their own competing services in the late 2010s.
facts.visualDescription: A split image: left side showing Netflix DVD mailers and a laptop in 2007, right side a modern 4K TV streaming interface with the red Netflix logo

facts.quizQuestion: Which two co-founders launched Netflix in 1997 as a subscription-based DVD-by-mail service that competed with Blockbuster's brick-and-mortar model?
facts.explanation: Reed Hastings and Marc Randolph co-founded Netflix in 1997. Legend says Hastings was inspired after paying a $40 late fee for a VHS copy of Apollo 13 at Blockbuster — though Hastings has said this story may be embellished. Netflix disrupted and ultimately killed the video rental store industry.
facts.visualDescription: A 1997 red envelope Netflix DVD mailer beside a blockbuster video store, a receipt showing a late fee circled in red

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Amazon Web Services launch its cloud computing infrastructure services, pioneering the modern cloud industry?
facts.explanation: Amazon Web Services launched its cloud computing services in March 2006 with services like S3 (storage) and EC2 (computing). This allowed businesses to rent computing resources on demand instead of buying servers. AWS now generates more profit for Amazon than its retail operations.
facts.visualDescription: A server room with glowing racks of equipment transforming into floating data clouds, the AWS orange logo hovering above

facts.quizQuestion: Usenet, the internet's original discussion forum system conceived in 1979, was created by which two Duke University graduate students?
facts.explanation: Tom Truscott and Jim Ellis conceived Usenet in 1979 at Duke University as a way to share news and messages between computer users. Established in 1980, Usenet predated the World Wide Web by over a decade and established the concept of topic-based online communities that later became forums and subreddits.
facts.visualDescription: A 1980 university terminal showing a scrolling list of newsgroup topics, a student adding a reply to an early discussion thread

facts.quizQuestion: Which social network, founded in 2003 and famous for letting users customize their profile pages with HTML, was the world's most visited website from 2005 to 2008 — before being dethroned by Facebook?
facts.explanation: Myspace, launched August 1, 2003, by Chris DeWolfe and Tom Anderson, was the dominant social network for several years. It was particularly influential in music discovery. News Corp bought it for $580 million in 2005 — one of the most expensive failed acquisitions in tech history. Justin Timberlake later became part-owner.
facts.visualDescription: A 2006 Myspace profile page with a custom background, an embedded music player, and a glittery animated GIF — peak mid-2000s internet aesthetic

facts.quizQuestion: What was the title of Alan Turing's landmark 1950 paper that introduced the 'imitation game' — later known as the Turing test?
facts.explanation: Alan Turing published 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' in 1950, opening with the question 'Can machines think?' He proposed the imitation game as a way to evaluate machine intelligence: if a human evaluator can't reliably distinguish a machine's responses from a human's, the machine passes. The test was later named the Turing test in his honor.
facts.visualDescription: A philosopher at a 1950s desk, a copy of the journal Mind open to a dense academic paper, a typewriter nearby

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Alan Turing propose the 'imitation game' (later called the Turing test) as a measure of machine intelligence?
facts.explanation: Alan Turing proposed the imitation game in his 1950 paper 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence.' The test asks whether a machine can exhibit intelligent behavior indistinguishable from a human. It remains one of the most discussed concepts in AI philosophy, even though modern AI systems have rendered it somewhat obsolete as a benchmark.
facts.visualDescription: A 1950 era typewriter with a philosophical paper emerging, a clock face showing the year on the wall behind it

facts.quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'artificial intelligence' at the 1956 Dartmouth Summer Research Project — an event considered the founding moment of AI as a field?
facts.explanation: John McCarthy coined 'artificial intelligence' at the 1956 Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence, which he organized along with Claude Shannon, Marvin Minsky, and Nathaniel Rochester. McCarthy also invented the Lisp programming language and is considered one of the founders of AI as a discipline.
facts.visualDescription: A 1956 summer conference at Dartmouth College, a chalkboard with 'Artificial Intelligence' written on it, a small group of scientists in short-sleeve shirts debating

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did the Dartmouth Summer Research Project take place — the workshop where 'artificial intelligence' was named as a new field?
facts.explanation: The Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence took place in the summer of 1956 in Hanover, New Hampshire. The workshop brought together ten researchers for six to eight weeks. While they didn't solve AI, the meeting established it as a serious academic field and named the discipline.
facts.visualDescription: A New England university campus in summer, a group of scientists gathered around a chalkboard, the words 'artificial intelligence' freshly written

facts.quizQuestion: Who created ELIZA at MIT in the mid-1960s — the chatbot that mimicked a psychotherapist so convincingly it alarmed even its own creator?
facts.explanation: Joseph Weizenbaum developed ELIZA at MIT from 1964 to 1967, publishing it in 1966. ELIZA's most famous persona was 'DOCTOR,' which simulated a Rogerian psychotherapist. Weizenbaum was disturbed when users formed emotional attachments to the program despite knowing it was just pattern-matching. He became a prominent AI critic.
facts.visualDescription: A 1960s MIT computing lab with a teletype terminal, a researcher watching a student having an earnest text 'conversation' with ELIZA

facts.quizQuestion: What critical 1973 report, commissioned by the British Science Research Council, triggered the first AI winter by concluding that AI had failed to live up to its promises?
facts.explanation: The Lighthill Report, authored by mathematician Sir James Lighthill and published in 1973, harshly criticized AI research in the UK, concluding it had produced no major discoveries and its promises were vastly overstated. It triggered dramatic funding cuts in the UK and set off the first AI winter (~1974-1980).
facts.visualDescription: A formal 1970s government document with a British seal, the title 'Artificial Intelligence: A General Survey' visible, stacks of cancelled research grants nearby

facts.quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'machine learning' in 1959, while developing a checkers-playing program at IBM that improved through practice?
facts.explanation: Arthur Samuel, a computer scientist at IBM, coined 'machine learning' in 1959, defining it as the ability of computers to learn without being explicitly programmed. His checkers program learned by playing thousands of games against itself, becoming a strong player — one of the earliest demonstrations of a self-improving AI system.
facts.visualDescription: A 1950s IBM mainframe room, a checkerboard displayed on a large monitor, a programmer watching the computer make an unexpected move

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented the perceptron in 1957 at Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory — the first artificial neural network model capable of supervised learning?
facts.explanation: Frank Rosenblatt invented the perceptron in 1957, implementing it as the Mark I Perceptron machine. It was a hardware device that could learn to classify images of simple shapes. Initial press coverage called it 'the embryo of an electronic computer that it is expected to walk, talk, see, write.' It became a direct ancestor of modern deep learning.
facts.visualDescription: A 1950s research lab with a large custom machine covered in wires and photocells, a researcher pointing at a camera assembly feeding visual input

acceptableAlternatives: David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton, and Ronald Williams
facts.quizQuestion: Which trio of researchers popularized backpropagation in their landmark 1986 Nature paper 'Learning representations by back-propagating errors'?
facts.explanation: David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton, and Ronald Williams published their influential 1986 paper in Nature that made backpropagation — the key algorithm for training multi-layer neural networks — practical and widely adopted. This enabled the training of the deeper networks that form the foundation of modern deep learning.
facts.visualDescription: A 1986 academic journal opened to a dense mathematics paper, a neural network diagram with arrows showing error gradients flowing backward

facts.quizQuestion: Support Vector Machines (SVMs), published in 1995 by Corinna Cortes and Vladimir Vapnik, were developed at which legendary research institution?
facts.explanation: Corinna Cortes and Vladimir Vapnik developed Support Vector Machines at AT&T Bell Laboratories. SVMs find the optimal separating hyperplane between classes in high-dimensional space and were state-of-the-art for classification problems before deep learning surpassed them. Bell Labs also produced the transistor, Unix, C, and many other foundational technologies.
facts.visualDescription: A Bell Labs research whiteboard with a geometric diagram showing two groups of points separated by a maximum-margin hyperplane

facts.quizQuestion: Who formalized and named the 'random forests' algorithm in a 2001 paper, creating one of the most widely used ensemble learning methods?
facts.explanation: Leo Breiman formalized and named random forests in his 2001 paper in the journal Machine Learning. Random forests build many decision trees on random subsets of data and average their predictions, producing more robust results than any single tree. Breiman's method extended Tin Kam Ho's 1995 random subspace method.
facts.visualDescription: A stylized forest of decision trees growing on a mathematical grid, each tree slightly different, converging arrows pointing to a single answer

facts.quizQuestion: AlexNet's breakthrough 2012 ImageNet victory was achieved by a team from which Canadian, led by Geoffrey Hinton?
facts.explanation: AlexNet was developed at the University of Toronto by Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, and Geoffrey Hinton. Their 2012 ImageNet win — reducing error rates from 26% to 15% — was so dramatic it triggered the modern deep learning revolution. Hinton, Krizhevsky, and Sutskever later joined Google.
facts.visualDescription: A Canadian university campus with a deep neural network diagram overlaid, a bar chart showing ImageNet error rates dramatically dropping in 2012

acceptableAlternatives: ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge
facts.quizQuestion: What annual image recognition competition did AlexNet win in 2012 by a stunning margin, triggering the modern deep learning revolution?
facts.explanation: AlexNet won the ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) in 2012 with a top-5 error rate of 15.3%, compared to 26.2% for the runner-up. The magnitude of the gap convinced the AI research community that deep convolutional neural networks were the future.
facts.visualDescription: A 2012 competition scoreboard with a massive gap between first place (AlexNet) and second place, surprised researchers looking at the results

facts.quizQuestion: IBM's Deep Blue made history in May 1997 by defeating which reigning world chess champion — the first time a computer beat a human champion under standard tournament conditions?
facts.explanation: IBM's Deep Blue defeated Garry Kasparov 3.5-2.5 in a six-game match in New York in May 1997. Deep Blue could evaluate approximately 200 million positions per second. The defeat was a cultural landmark — Kasparov accused IBM of cheating and demanded a rematch, which IBM refused, and then retired Deep Blue.
facts.visualDescription: A tense chess match under bright lights — a Russian grandmaster staring intensely at the board across from a tall black IBM computer terminal

facts.quizQuestion: IBM's Watson AI defeated human champions Ken Jennings and Brad Rutter in February 2011 on what television quiz show?
facts.explanation: IBM's Watson beat Jeopardy! champions Ken Jennings and Brad Rutter in a televised three-game match in February 2011, winning the $1 million prize (which IBM donated to charity). Watson had to understand natural language 'answers' and formulate correct 'questions' — a much harder challenge than chess. Ken Jennings wrote 'I for one welcome our new computer overlords' on his Final Jeopardy answer.
facts.visualDescription: A TV game show stage with three podiums: two human champions on the sides and a large IBM Watson computer terminal in the center, colored buzzers lit up
distractors: Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?
Are You Smarter Than a 5th Grader?

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Google DeepMind's AlphaGo defeat world Go champion Lee Sedol 4-1, arriving a decade ahead of expert predictions?
facts.explanation: AlphaGo defeated Lee Sedol in March 2016, years before experts expected a computer to beat a top human player. Go has more possible board positions than atoms in the observable universe, making it far harder than chess. AlphaGo used deep reinforcement learning and Monte Carlo tree search — a radically different approach from Deep Blue's brute-force search.
facts.visualDescription: A Go board with black and white stones in the final positions of a historic game, a split screen showing the AI system interface on one side

facts.quizQuestion: Which AI research company, founded by Demis Hassabis and acquired by Google, created AlphaGo and AlphaFold?
facts.explanation: Google DeepMind (originally DeepMind Technologies, founded in London in 2010 by Demis Hassabis, Shane Legg, and Mustafa Suleyman) was acquired by Google in 2014. It created AlphaGo, AlphaFold, and many other landmark AI systems. In 2024 Hassabis shared the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for AlphaFold's contributions.
facts.visualDescription: A modern London AI research lab, neural network visualizations on large monitors, a Go board and protein structure model on the same desk

facts.quizQuestion: What 50-year-old grand challenge in biology did DeepMind's AlphaFold 2 essentially solve at the CASP14 competition in November 2020?
facts.explanation: AlphaFold 2 achieved accuracy in predicting how proteins fold into 3D shapes comparable to expensive experimental methods. This 'protein folding problem' had stumped scientists for 50 years because a protein's shape determines its function — knowing the shape enables drug discovery and disease understanding.
facts.visualDescription: A 3D ribbon diagram of a protein structure glowing in blue and orange, a researcher comparing it to an experimental X-ray crystallography image with astonishment

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did DeepMind co-founder Demis Hassabis and John Jumper share the Nobel Prize in Chemistry for AlphaFold's contributions to protein structure research?
facts.explanation: Demis Hassabis and John Jumper of Google DeepMind shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry (along with David Baker of the University of Washington) for computational protein design and protein structure prediction. This marked a historic recognition of AI research at the highest level of scientific achievement.
facts.visualDescription: A Nobel Prize medal against a backdrop of protein structure ribbon diagrams and a DNA helix, Stockholm concert hall in the background

facts.quizQuestion: How many parameters did GPT-3, OpenAI's 2020 large language model, contain — shocking the research community with its scale?
facts.explanation: GPT-3 contained 175 billion parameters when released in 2020, roughly 100 times more than its predecessor GPT-2. The scale was so large it could perform tasks it was never explicitly trained on, just by reading a few examples — a capability researchers called 'few-shot learning.'
facts.visualDescription: A visualization of neural network nodes branching to countless connections, labeled '175 billion parameters', server racks glowing in the background

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did OpenAI release ChatGPT, which reached 100 million users in just two months — the fastest consumer tech product to do so?
facts.explanation: ChatGPT launched November 30, 2022, and reached 100 million users in two months — far faster than Instagram (2.5 years) or TikTok (9 months). It was built on GPT-3.5 and used reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to make it safer and more helpful than its predecessor.
facts.visualDescription: A smartphone screen showing the ChatGPT interface with a question being typed, a line chart in the background showing user growth rocketing skyward

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did OpenAI first announce DALL-E, the text-to-image AI model that could generate novel images from written descriptions?
facts.explanation: DALL-E was first announced January 5, 2021. The name is a portmanteau of the artist Salvador Dalí and the Pixar robot WALL-E. It demonstrated that neural networks could generate surprisingly creative and detailed images from text prompts, launching the generative image AI era.
facts.visualDescription: An AI-generated surrealist painting of an armchair shaped like an avocado — the famous original DALL-E demo — floating in a clean white gallery

facts.quizQuestion: What is the title of the 2017 Google paper that introduced the transformer architecture — the foundation for GPT, BERT, and essentially all modern large language models?
facts.explanation: 'Attention Is All You Need' was published in 2017 by eight Google researchers. It introduced the transformer architecture, replacing recurrent neural networks with self-attention mechanisms. The title is now famous for its boldness — and accuracy. The paper has been cited hundreds of thousands of times.
facts.visualDescription: An academic paper with the bold title 'Attention Is All You Need' on the cover, neural network attention heatmaps visualizing which words attend to other words in a sentence

facts.quizQuestion: The transformer architecture replaced recurrent neural networks with what key mechanism, allowing it to relate any two positions in a sequence regardless of distance?
facts.explanation: The self-attention mechanism allows each position in a sequence to attend to every other position simultaneously, rather than processing sequences step-by-step. This made transformers massively parallelizable (fast to train) and gave them much better performance on long-range dependencies in language.
facts.visualDescription: A diagram of a transformer block showing multiple arrows connecting each word token to every other word token with varying weights highlighted

facts.quizQuestion: Who invented Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) in June 2014, reportedly working out the idea over drinks and implementing it the same night?
facts.explanation: Ian Goodfellow invented GANs in June 2014. The story goes that he came up with the idea of pitting two networks against each other (a generator and a discriminator) at a bar after a friend's PhD defense. He went home, wrote the code, and it worked on the first try — a remarkable feat. GANs enabled unprecedented realistic synthetic image generation.
facts.visualDescription: Two neural networks illustrated as rival artists — one sketching fake images, one examining them under a magnifying glass — in an adversarial loop

facts.quizQuestion: Who pioneered convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in 1989, training them to recognize handwritten zip codes using backpropagation?
facts.explanation: Yann LeCun demonstrated CNNs in a 1989 paper on handwritten zip code recognition. Inspired by the visual cortex, CNNs use learned convolutional filters to detect local patterns in images. His later LeNet-5 system (1998) was deployed to read checks at banks. CNNs became the dominant architecture for computer vision.
facts.visualDescription: A 1989 research lab with a paper going through a document scanner, a CNN visualization on screen showing feature maps detecting edges and curves

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Yann LeCun share the Turing Award — often called the 'Nobel Prize of computing' — with Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio for deep learning contributions?
facts.explanation: Yann LeCun, Geoffrey Hinton, and Yoshua Bengio shared the 2018 ACM Turing Award, often called the Nobel Prize of computing. The three are collectively known as the 'Godfathers of Deep Learning' for their decades of work on neural networks during times when the approach was deeply unfashionable.
facts.visualDescription: Three researchers on a stage receiving awards, a neural network diagram projected behind them, the text 'ACM Turing Award' visible on the podium

facts.quizQuestion: Who created the ImageNet dataset at Stanford — the massive labeled image database that enabled the 2012 AlexNet breakthrough and launched the modern deep learning era?
facts.explanation: Fei-Fei Li and her colleagues created ImageNet starting in 2006, using Amazon Mechanical Turk to label millions of images across thousands of categories. The ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) became the benchmark for computer vision — and AlexNet's 2012 victory on it sparked the deep learning revolution.
facts.visualDescription: A Stanford professor surrounded by thousands of tiny labeled images projected on a large screen — dogs, cars, chairs, all tagged with category names

facts.quizQuestion: What AI approach — which encoded human expertise as hand-crafted rules — collapsed in the late 1980s, triggering the second AI winter (~1987-2000)?
facts.explanation: Expert systems were AI programs encoding domain expertise as thousands of hand-crafted 'if-then' rules. They were popular in the 1980s but proved brittle, expensive to maintain, and unable to handle the complexity of the real world. When businesses stopped investing in them, AI funding dried up, beginning the second winter.
facts.visualDescription: A 1980s mainframe terminal running a medical diagnosis expert system, displaying rows of coded rules and decision trees, a frustrated programmer adding yet another rule

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Demis Hassabis co-found Google DeepMind — the London-based AI research company that created AlphaGo and AlphaFold?
facts.explanation: Demis Hassabis, Shane Legg, and Mustafa Suleyman co-founded DeepMind Technologies in London in 2010. Google acquired it in 2014 for approximately $500 million. DeepMind's string of breakthroughs — from beating Atari games to solving protein folding — established it as one of the world's foremost AI research organizations.
facts.visualDescription: A stylish London office in 2010, neural network visualizations on large screens, the DeepMind logo being designed on a whiteboard

facts.quizQuestion: Which AI research company released GPT-3 in 2020 and ChatGPT in 2022, triggering a global wave of generative AI development?
facts.explanation: OpenAI, founded in 2015 as a non-profit by Elon Musk, Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, and others, released GPT-3 in 2020 and ChatGPT on November 30, 2022. ChatGPT's viral success turned OpenAI into one of the world's most valuable AI companies and spurred massive investment in generative AI.
facts.visualDescription: The OpenAI logo on a modern tech building, a ChatGPT conversation window on a laptop screen, users typing questions worldwide connected by glowing lines

facts.quizQuestion: What classification algorithm, developed by Cortes and Vapnik at Bell Labs and published in 1995, finds the maximum-margin hyperplane between two classes of data?
facts.explanation: Support Vector Machines (SVMs) find the optimal decision boundary — the hyperplane that maximizes the margin between two classes in feature space. They can be extended to non-linear classification using the 'kernel trick.' SVMs were state-of-the-art for many classification tasks in the late 1990s and 2000s before deep learning surpassed them.
facts.visualDescription: A 2D scatter plot with two clusters of points (circles and crosses) separated by a bold line with maximum margin dashed lines on either side

facts.quizQuestion: Deep Blue — the chess computer that defeated Garry Kasparov in 1997 — was built by which technology company?
facts.explanation: IBM built Deep Blue, which could evaluate approximately 200 million chess positions per second using specialized hardware. IBM used Deep Blue's victory as a major marketing triumph. After retiring Deep Blue following the 1997 match, IBM moved on to new AI challenges, eventually building Watson.
facts.visualDescription: The IBM logo on a tall black computer cabinet labeled 'Deep Blue', a chess board with pieces mid-game in front of it, Kasparov's face reflected in the machine's dark surface

facts.quizQuestion: AlphaGo made history in 2016 by becoming the first AI to defeat a world champion at what ancient board game, considered harder than chess?
facts.explanation: AlphaGo defeated world champion Lee Sedol at Go in March 2016. Go is played on a 19x19 board with more possible positions than atoms in the observable universe, making brute-force search impossible. AlphaGo used deep reinforcement learning and Monte Carlo tree search to evaluate positions intuitively, more like a human master.
facts.visualDescription: A Go board with black and white stones, the massive branching game tree of possible moves illustrated behind it, dwarfing the comparatively simple chess tree

facts.quizQuestion: John McCarthy — who coined 'artificial intelligence' at the 1956 Dartmouth conference — also invented which influential programming language still used in AI research today?
facts.explanation: John McCarthy invented Lisp (LISt Processing) in 1958, making it the second-oldest high-level programming language still in use. Lisp was designed for symbolic computation and became foundational in AI research. It introduced many concepts now common in programming: automatic garbage collection, dynamic typing, and higher-order functions.
facts.visualDescription: A 1960s computer lab with a Lisp interpreter running on a terminal, parentheses-heavy code filling the screen, a list structure diagram on the blackboard

facts.quizQuestion: Hewlett-Packard's founding garage — now a California Historical Landmark dubbed the 'birthplace of Silicon Valley' — was located in what city?
facts.explanation: Bill Hewlett and David Packard started Hewlett-Packard in 1939 in a one-car garage at 367 Addison Avenue in Palo Alto, California. The garage is preserved as California Historical Landmark #976 and is often credited as the starting point for Silicon Valley's tech culture.
facts.visualDescription: A small white wooden garage on a quiet residential street, a vintage audio oscillator on a workbench inside, two engineers in 1930s attire tinkering

facts.quizQuestion: HP's first commercial product, sold to Walt Disney Studios for use in the film Fantasia, was what kind of electronic instrument?
facts.explanation: HP's first product was the HP 200A audio oscillator, which generated precise audio tones. Walt Disney Studios purchased several for testing theater speaker systems for the film Fantasia (1940). The oscillator sold for $54.40 — underselling competitors who charged hundreds more.
facts.visualDescription: A vintage wooden-cased electronic instrument on a workbench, oscilloscope waveforms on a small screen, old film reels labeled 'Fantasia' nearby

facts.quizQuestion: IBM was formed in 1911 under what unwieldy original name before being renamed 'International Business Machines' in 1924?
facts.explanation: IBM traces its origins to 1911 when three companies merged to form the Computing-Tabulating-Recording Company (CTR) in Endicott, New York. In 1924, under CEO Thomas Watson Sr., it was renamed IBM. Watson Sr. drove the company's transformation into a global technology powerhouse.
facts.visualDescription: A 1911 company merger document with three company names crossing through to a single new name, early punch card machines lined up in a factory

facts.quizQuestion: Which two co-founders of Intel also previously co-founded Fairchild Semiconductor, helping birth Silicon Valley's chip industry?
facts.explanation: Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore co-founded Intel on July 18, 1968, after leaving Fairchild Semiconductor. Noyce co-invented the integrated circuit (with Jack Kilby). Moore's Law — Moore's 1965 observation that transistor counts double roughly every two years — remains one of tech's most famous predictions. Their original Intel name was 'NM Electronics.'
facts.visualDescription: Two engineers in clean-room suits in a 1960s semiconductor lab, silicon wafers on a tray, circuit diagrams covering the wall behind them

facts.quizQuestion: Which Intel co-founder's 1965 observation — that transistor counts roughly double every two years — became known as 'the concept's Law'?
facts.explanation: Gordon Moore published his observation in a 1965 Electronics Magazine article. He noticed transistors were doubling roughly every year (later revised to every two years). Moore's Law became a self-fulfilling prophecy as the semiconductor industry set roadmaps around it. It held remarkably well for over 50 years before physical limits began to slow progress.
facts.visualDescription: A logarithmic graph of transistor counts per chip from 1970 to 2020 showing an exponential climb, with Intel chip milestones marked along the curve

facts.quizQuestion: Microsoft was founded in 1975 by Bill Gates and Paul Allen — but NOT in Washington state. What city was it actually founded in?
facts.explanation: Microsoft was founded in Albuquerque, New Mexico in 1975, where Bill Gates and Paul Allen were working with MITS on the Altair 8800 microcomputer. Microsoft only relocated to the Seattle, Washington area in 1979. The Albuquerque origins are often overlooked in favor of the company's later Pacific Northwest identity.
facts.visualDescription: A 1975 New Mexico office building with a small startup sign reading 'Microsoft', two young men in 70s attire working at early microcomputers

facts.quizQuestion: Which two childhood friends co-founded Microsoft in 1975, bonding over their shared obsession with programming computers at their Seattle high school?
facts.explanation: Bill Gates and Paul Allen co-founded Microsoft in 1975. They had met as students at Lakeside School in Seattle, where they spent hours programming on the school's computer terminals. Their first major product was a BASIC interpreter for the Altair 8800. Gates dropped out of Harvard; Allen left Washington State University to join him.
facts.visualDescription: Two teenage programmers at a 1970s computer terminal in a school computer room, lines of BASIC code scrolling on a green-screen monitor

facts.quizQuestion: Apple was co-founded by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and a forgotten third co-founder who sold his 10% stake back for $800 — what was his name?
facts.explanation: Ronald Wayne co-founded Apple on April 1, 1976, and designed Apple's first logo (a Newton sitting under an apple tree). He sold his 10% stake back to Jobs and Wozniak for $800, fearing liability. That stake would have been worth over $350 billion by 2022. Wayne later said he didn't regret it given what he knew at the time.
facts.visualDescription: An old hand-drawn Apple logo of Newton under an apple tree, a contract document with three signatures, two men celebrating while a third quietly walks away

facts.quizQuestion: On what famous calendar date in 1976 was Apple Computer Company formally incorporated — leading many to assume it was a joke?
facts.explanation: Apple was incorporated on April 1, 1976 — April Fools' Day. Steve Jobs reportedly chose the date either accidentally or because he enjoyed the irony. The company's first product was the Apple I, a hand-assembled computer sold as a bare circuit board for $666.66.
facts.visualDescription: A calendar open to April 1st, an early Apple I circuit board beside it, Steve Jobs' handwritten incorporation documents visible
